items.label: Half-Open Intervals as Open Sets in a Subspace
items.statement:
Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2, and let 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ. Then sets of the form [0, 𝑎) and (𝑎, 1] with 0 < 𝑎 < 1 are open in 𝐼 but not in ℝ.

The following theorem describes how we may extract a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴 from the basis of a topology on 𝑋.
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2, and let 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ. Then sets of the form [0, 𝑎) and (𝑎, 1] with 0 < 𝑎 < 1 are open in 𝐼 but not in ℝ.
items.intuition: Illustrates half-open intervals as open sets in a subspace in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.4

items.label: Bases for Subspace Topologies
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. If 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋, then the collection

𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∣ 𝐵 ∈ }

is a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴.

Proof. We need to prove that 𝐴 is a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴. We first prove that 𝐴 is a basis for a topology on 𝐴, and then that the topology generated by 𝐴 equals the subspace topology on 𝐴. First note that each 𝐵 ∈  is open in 𝑋, and so, each 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∈ 𝐴 is open in 𝐴. For each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a basis element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵. Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑋, there must be a basis element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵. Hence, B1 holds. Now let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ (𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ) where 𝐵1 and 𝐵2 are basis elements of . Since  is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, it follows that there is a basis element 𝐵3 of  such that 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐴 ∩ (𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ). Hence, B2 is satisfied. Thus 𝐴 is a basis for a topology on 𝐴. Let 𝐴 be the subspace topology on 𝐴. We want to prove that the topology  ′ generated by 𝐴 is equal to 𝐴 . If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 , then, using the fact that  is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, we have 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 . Thus 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈  ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10 we know that  ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of 𝐴 . Hence, if 𝑊 ∈  ′ then 𝑊 equals a union of elements of 𝐴 . Since each element of 𝐴 belongs to 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a topology, 𝑊 also belongs to 𝐴 .

We end this section with an alternative description of the subspace topology. Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑇 be a set. There do exist topologies on 𝑇 that make 𝑓 ∶ 𝑇 → 𝑋 continuous, e.g., the discrete topology. If 𝑓 is the intersection of all topologies on 𝑇 such that 𝑓 is continuous, then 𝑓 is the coarsest topology for which 𝑓 is continuous and

𝑓 = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋}.

From this we may define the subspace topology as follows: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be subset of 𝑋. The subspace topology on 𝐴 is then the coarsest topology on 𝐴 for which the inclusion 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋, given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴, is continuous. This coincides with our previous definition as 𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 for any subset 𝑈 of 𝑋. Thus

𝑖 = {𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋},

and hence, 𝑖 = 𝐴 . The following theorem describes a universal property for the subspace topology.

32 5.1. Subspaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. If 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋, then the collection 𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∣ 𝐵 ∈ }

items.label: Universal Property of the Subspace Topology
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the subspace topology on 𝐴 is the only topology on 𝐴 with the following universal property: for every topo- logical space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴.

𝑋 𝑖◦𝑓 𝑖

𝑌 𝐴 𝑓

Proof. We will first prove that the subspace topology 𝐴 has the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous. Then we will prove that 𝐴 is the only topology on 𝐴 with this property. Consider 𝐴 as a subspace of 𝑋. Assume that 𝑓 is continuous. Since the inclusion map 𝑖 is con- tinuous (with 𝐴 given the subspace topology), and the composition of two continuous maps is again continuous, cf. Theorem 3.16, it follows that 𝑖◦𝑓 is continuous. Now assume that 𝑖◦𝑓 is continuous. Let 𝑉 be an open set in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑉 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 for some open set 𝑈 in 𝑋. Since

𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑖−1 (𝑈 )) = (𝑖◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑈 )

is open in 𝑌 by continuity of 𝑖◦𝑓 , it follows that 𝑓 is continuous. Thus the subspace topology 𝐴 has the desired property. Let  ′ be a topology on 𝐴 with the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous. We must show that 𝐴 =  ′ . Let  be the topology on 𝑋, and let 𝐴 be given the topology  ′ . First let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with the subspace topology. Then for 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴, 𝐴 ) → (𝐴,  ′ ), we have 𝑖◦ id = 𝑖 ∶ (𝐴, 𝐴 ) → (𝑋,  ) which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

(𝑋,  ) 𝑖◦ id 𝑖

(𝐴, 𝐴 ) (𝐴,  ′ ) id

Thus any 𝑉 ∈  ′ must also be an element of 𝐴 , and so,  ′ ⊆ 𝐴 . Secondly let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with  ′ as its topology. Then, clearly, 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝐴,  ′ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that 𝑖◦ id = 𝑖 ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝑋,  ) is continuous.

(𝑋,  ) 𝑖◦ id 𝑖

(𝐴,  ′ ) (𝐴,  ′ ) id

Thus for any 𝑈 ∈  , we have 𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈  ′ . That is, 𝐴 ⊆  ′ . Hence, 𝐴 =  ′ .

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 33

5.2 Products Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If we want to give the product 𝑋 × 𝑌 a topology, a first approach might be to take the collection  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 } as the topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 . However,  is not a topology. The union of two elements of  is not necessarily of the form 𝑈 × 𝑉 for some 𝑈 open in 𝑋 and some 𝑉 open in 𝑌 . See Figure 5.3.

𝑌

𝑉1 𝑈1 × 𝑉1

𝑉2 𝑈2 × 𝑉2

𝑋 𝑈1 𝑈2

Figure 5.3: The collection  of all products of open sets in 𝑋 and in 𝑌 is not a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

We can remedy the situation by taking  as a basis instead. The topology generated from this basis is what we will take to be the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the subspace topology on 𝐴 is the only topology on 𝐴 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴. 𝑋 𝑖◦𝑓 𝑖
items.intuition: States a main result about universal property of the subspace topology; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.

𝑋 𝑖◦𝑓 𝑖

items.label: Product topology
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. The product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 is the topology generated by the basis  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. The product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 is the topology generated by the basis

 = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }.
items.intuition: Defines product topology and fixes terminology used later in basis, open-sets, product.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.7

items.label: Rectangles Form a Basis for the Product Topology
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the collection

 = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }

is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

Proof. Let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . We need to show that there is a basis element 𝑈 × 𝑉 ∈  such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Since 𝑋 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑌 is open in 𝑌 , we simply take 𝑈 = 𝑋 and 𝑉 = 𝑌 . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ (𝑈1 × 𝑉1 ) ∩ (𝑈2 × 𝑉2 ) where 𝑈1 × 𝑉1 , 𝑈2 × 𝑉2 ∈ . Since (𝑈1 × 𝑉1 ) ∩ (𝑈2 × 𝑉2 ) = (𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ) × (𝑉1 ∩ 𝑉2 ), and 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 and 𝑉1 ∩ 𝑉2 are open in 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively, it follows by letting 𝑈3 = 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 and 𝑉3 = 𝑉1 ∩𝑉2 that there is a basis element 𝑈3 ×𝑉3 ∈  such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈3 ×𝑉3 ⊆ (𝑈1 ×𝑉1 )∩(𝑈2 ×𝑉2 ). Hence, B2 holds. Thus  is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

34 5.2. Products

The basis described in Lemma 5.8 is relatively large; it consists of pairs of every open set 𝑈 in 𝑋 and every open set 𝑉 in 𝑌 . The following theorem describes a smaller basis for the product topology based on bases rather than whole topologies.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the collection  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }
items.intuition: Records an auxiliary result about rectangles form a basis for the product topology that supports later statements or proofs.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.8

items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the collection  = {𝑈 × 𝑉 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 }

items.label: Product Basis from Bases on Factors
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑋 is a basis for the topology on 𝑋 and 𝑌 is a basis for the topology on 𝑌 , then the collection

𝑋×𝑌 = {𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ∣ 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 }

is a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.5 and adapt them to our current setting. First note that each 𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑌 as each 𝐵𝑋 is open in 𝑋 and each 𝐵𝑌 is open in 𝑌 . Let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Using the fact that 𝑋 and 𝑌 are bases for topologies on 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively, there are basis elements 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ⊆ 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ (𝐵𝑋,1 × 𝐵𝑌 ,1 ) ∩ (𝐵𝑋,2 × 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ) where 𝐵𝑋,1 , 𝐵𝑋,2 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ,1 , 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ∈ 𝑌 . Since (𝐵𝑋,1 × 𝐵𝑌 ,1 ) ∩ (𝐵𝑋,2 × 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ) = (𝐵𝑋,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑋,2 ) × (𝐵𝑌 ,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ) and 𝑋 and 𝑌 are bases for the topologies on 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively, it follows that there are basis elements 𝐵𝑋,3 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ,3 ∈ 𝑌 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵𝑋,3 ⊆ 𝐵𝑋,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑋,2 , 𝑦 ∈ 𝐵𝑌 ,3 ⊆ 𝐵𝑌 ,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑌 ,2 and (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝐵𝑋,3 × 𝐵𝑌 ,3 ⊆ (𝐵𝑋,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑋,2 ) × (𝐵𝑌 ,1 ∩ 𝐵𝑌 ,2 ). Thus B2 holds. Hence, 𝑋×𝑌 is a basis for a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 . Let 𝑋×𝑌 be the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 . We want to prove that the topology  ′ generated by 𝑋×𝑌 is equal to 𝑋×𝑌 . Let 𝑊 ∈ 𝑋×𝑌 , and let (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑊 . Then there is an open set 𝑈 in 𝑋 and an open set 𝑉 in 𝑌 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑊 , cf. Theorem 4.9. Since 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑋 is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, it follows that there is a basis element 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . Likewise, there is a basis element 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 such that 𝑦 ∈ 𝐵𝑌 ⊆ 𝑉 . Thus (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ⊆ 𝑊 , and so, 𝑊 ∈  ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10, we know that  ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of 𝑋×𝑌 . Hence, if 𝑊 ∈  ′ then 𝑊 equals a union of elements of 𝑋×𝑌 . Since each element of 𝑋×𝑌 belongs to 𝑋×𝑌 and 𝑋×𝑌 is a topology, 𝑊 also belongs to 𝑋×𝑌 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑋 is a basis for the topology on 𝑋 and 𝑌 is a basis for the topology on 𝑌 , then the collection 𝑋×𝑌 = {𝐵𝑋 × 𝐵𝑌 ∣ 𝐵𝑋 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝐵𝑌 ∈ 𝑌 }
items.intuition: States a main result about product basis from bases on factors; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.

items.label: Computing a Product Topology on Finite Spaces
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} and 𝑌 = {1, 2, 3} with topologies 𝑋 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} and 𝑌 = {∅, {1}, {1, 2}, 𝑌 }, respectively. Then the collection { 𝑋×𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1}, {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1, 2}, } {𝑎, 𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × 𝑌

is a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} and 𝑌 = {1, 2, 3} with topologies 𝑋 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} and 𝑌 = {∅, {1}, {1, 2}, 𝑌 }, respectively. Then the collection { 𝑋×𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏} × {1}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1}, {𝑎, 𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏} × {1, 2}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × {1, 2}, } {𝑎, 𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏} × 𝑌 , {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒} × 𝑌 is a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .
items.intuition: Illustrates computing a product topology on finite spaces in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.10

items.label: Open Rectangles in the Euclidean Plane
items.statement:
Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2. Then the collection ℝ2 = {(𝑎, 𝑏) × (𝑐, 𝑑) ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑏, 𝑐 < 𝑑} of open rectangular regions in ℝ2 is a basis for the product topology on ℝ2 = ℝ × ℝ, as a basis for the standard topology of ℝ is the collection of open intervals of the form (𝑎, 𝑏) where 𝑎 < 𝑏.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 35

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.11

items.label: The Circle as a Subspace of the Plane
items.statement:
Let 𝑆 1 denote the circle {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 = 1} seen as a subspace of ℝ2 (with the standard topology), i.e., 𝑆 1 is given the subspace topology. A basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 is illustrated in Figure 5.4. Note that the surface depicted in Figure 5.4 is homeomorphic to 𝑇 2 ; it is the surface of revolution generated by revolving a circle, say of radius 1 in the 𝑥𝑧-plane with center (2, 0, 0), about an axis, e.g., the 𝑧-axis.

Figure 5.4: An illustration of a basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 .

We end this section with an alternative description of the product topology. The map pr 1 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑋 given by pr 1 (𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑥 for (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 is called the projection of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto 𝑋. Similarly, the map pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑌 → 𝑌 given by pr 2 (𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑦 for (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 is called the projection of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto 𝑌 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑆 1 denote the circle {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 = 1} seen as a subspace of ℝ2 (with the standard topology), i.e., 𝑆 1 is given the subspace topology. A basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 is illustrated in Figure 5.4. Note that the surface depicted in Figure 5.4 is homeomorphic to 𝑇 2 ; it is the surface of revolution generated by revolving a circle, say of radius 1 in the 𝑥𝑧-plane with center (2, 0, 0), about an axis, e.g., the 𝑧-axis. Figure 5.4: An illustration of a basis element 𝐵 for the product topology on 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 .
items.intuition: Illustrates the circle as a subspace of the plane in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.12

items.label: Universal Property of the Product Topology
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Let pr 1 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋×𝑌 → 𝑌 be the projections of 𝑋 × 𝑌 onto its first and second factors, respectively. The product topology is the only topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous.

𝑋×𝑌 𝑋×𝑌 𝑓 𝑓 pr 1 pr 2

𝑍 𝑋 𝑍 𝑌 pr 1 ◦𝑓 pr 2 ◦𝑓

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.6 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the product topology 𝑋×𝑌 has the universal property that for every topolog- ical space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous. Let 𝑋 × 𝑌 be given the product topology, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 be continuous. Since pr −1 1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑈 × 𝑌 for an open set 𝑈 in 𝑋 and 𝑌 is open in 𝑌 , it follows that pr 1 is continuous. Likewise, pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 ×

36 5.2. Products

𝑌 → 𝑌 is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 both pr 1 ◦𝑓 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 are continuous. Now assume that pr 1 ◦𝑓 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 are continuous. Let 𝑈 × 𝑉 be a subset of 𝑋 × 𝑌 where 𝑈 is an open set in 𝑋 and 𝑉 is an open set in 𝑌 . Since 𝑈 × 𝑉 = pr −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ pr −1 (𝑉 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 × 𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (pr −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ pr −1 (𝑉 )) = 𝑓 −1 (pr −1 (𝑈 )) ∩ 𝑓 −1 (pr −1 (𝑉 )) = (pr 1 ◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑈 ) ∩ (pr 2 ◦𝑓 )−1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑍 by continuity of pr 1 ◦𝑓 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 , and subsets of the form 𝑈 × 𝑉 form a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 , cf. Lemma 5.8, it follows that 𝑓 is continuous. Let  be a topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the universal property that for every topological space 𝑍 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑍 → 𝑌 are continuous. We must show that 𝑋×𝑌 =  . Let 𝑋 be the topology on 𝑋 and 𝑌 be the topology on 𝑌 , and let 𝑋 × 𝑌 be given the topology  . First let 𝑍 = 𝑋 × 𝑌 with the product topology. Then for 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) → (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ), we have pr 1 ◦ id = pr 1 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) → (𝑋, 𝑋 ) and pr 2 ◦ id = pr 2 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑋×𝑌 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) which are both continuous. Thus by the universal property id is continuous.

Hence, any 𝑊 ∈  must also be an element of 𝑋×𝑌 , and so,  ⊆ 𝑋×𝑌 . Secondly let 𝑍 = 𝑋 ×𝑌 with  as its topology. Then, clearly, 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝑋 ×𝑌 ,  ) → (𝑋 ×𝑌 ,  ) is continuous. Thus by the universal property it follows that both pr 1 ◦ id = pr 1 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) → (𝑋, 𝑋 ) and pr 2 ◦ id = pr 2 ∶ (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) → (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) are continuous.

(𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) (𝑋, 𝑋 ) (𝑋 × 𝑌 ,  ) (𝑌 , 𝑌 ) pr 1 ◦ id pr 2 ◦ id

Thus for each 𝑈 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑉 ∈ 𝑌 , we have pr −1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑈 × 𝑌 ∈  and pr −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑋 × 𝑉 ∈  , and so, 𝑈 × 𝑉 = pr −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ pr −1 (𝑉 ) ∈  . Hence, 𝑋×𝑌 ⊆  . Thus 𝑋×𝑌 =  .

We can extend our discussion of the product topology from 𝑋 × 𝑌 to 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 where each 𝑋𝑖 ∏ is a topological space. If we are to extend to the product 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , which we can think of as the set of maps ⋃ 𝑓 ∶ Λ → 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 where 𝑓 (𝜆) ∈ 𝑋𝜆 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ, of an indexed family {𝑋𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ of topological spaces we ∏ ∏ may proceed in two ways. We may equip 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 with the topology generated by the basis 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 where ∏ 𝑈𝜆 is open in 𝑋𝜆 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. This is known as the box topology. We may also equip 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 with the ⋃ topology generated by the subbasis  = 𝜇∈Λ {pr −1 𝜇 (𝑈𝜇 ) ∣ 𝑈𝜇 is open in 𝑋𝜇 }. This is known as the product ∏𝑛 topology. For finite products 𝑖=1 𝑋𝑖 the two topologies are the same. Also, the box topology is, in general, finer than the product topology. Finally, several results regarding finite products may be extended to arbitrary products when using the product topology but not the box topology.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 37

5.3 Quotient spaces Let 𝑋 be a topological space. In Section 5.1, we discussed how to define the coarsest possible topology on a subset 𝐴 of 𝑋 such that the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is continuous. This is known as the subspace topology. If we let 𝐴 be a set which is not necessarily a subset of 𝑋 and we consider a surjective map 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴, the quotient topology is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous. The torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 (see Figure 5.4) can be constructed by taking a rectangle and “gluing” its edges together in an appropriate way as shown in Figure 5.5. Such a construction involves the concept of quotient topology.

items.label: Equivalence classes
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let ∼ be an equivalence relation on 𝑋. The equivalence class of 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 is the subset [𝑥] = {𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦}
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let ∼ be an equivalence relation on 𝑋. The equivalence class of 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 is the subset

[𝑥] = {𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 ∣ 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦}

of 𝑋. Let 𝑋∕∼ = {[𝑥] ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} be the set of equivalence classes.

By definition, 𝑥 ∈ [𝑥] for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and [𝑥] = [𝑦] if and only if 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦. Moreover, two equivalence classes [𝑥1 ] and [𝑥2 ] are either disjoint or equal. Finally, the union of all equivalence classes equal 𝑋.
items.intuition: Defines equivalence classes and fixes terminology used later in sets.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.14

items.label: Quotient Topology Is a Topology
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝐴 be sets, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. Then the map

𝜑 ∶ 𝑋∕∼ → 𝐴

given by 𝜑([𝑥]) = 𝜋(𝑥), where 𝑥1 ∼ 𝑥2 if and only if 𝜋(𝑥1 ) = 𝜋(𝑥2 ), is a bijection.

Proof. The map is well-defined since [𝑥1 ] = [𝑥2 ] only if 𝑥1 ∼ 𝑥2 , and so, 𝜋(𝑥1 ) = 𝜋(𝑥2 ) by definition of the equivalence relation. It is injective since 𝜑([𝑥1 ]) = 𝜑([𝑥2 ]) implies 𝜋(𝑥1 ) = 𝜋(𝑥2 ), and so, 𝑥1 ∼ 𝑥2 , i.e., [𝑥1 ] = [𝑥2 ]. Finally, it is surjective since 𝜋 is; every element of 𝐴 is of the form 𝜋(𝑥) = 𝜑([𝑥]) for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.

Thus by Lemma 5.15 we can, up to a bijection, go back and forth between equivalence relations on 𝑋 and surjective maps 𝑋 → 𝐴.

items.label: Quotient space
items.formal_statement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $A$ be a set, and let $\pi:X\to A$ be surjective. The quotient topology on $A$ induced by $\pi$ consists of those subsets $U\subseteq A$ for which $\pi^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$; with this topology, $A$ is called a quotient space.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. The quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is the collection of subsets 𝑈 of 𝐴 such that 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑋. We say that 𝜋 is a quotient map if 𝐴 is given the quotient topology, and we call 𝐴 the quotient space.

In other words, 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is quotient map if it is surjective and a subset 𝑈 of 𝐴 is open in 𝐴 if and only if 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑋. Equivalently, 𝜋 is a quotient map if it is surjective and 𝑈 is closed in 𝐴 if and only if 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is closed in 𝑋. Clearly, a quotient map is continuous.
items.intuition: A quotient space is built by declaring a subset of the target open exactly when its full preimage under the quotient map is open.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.16

items.label: Universal Property of the Quotient Topology
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. Then the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is a topology and it is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous.

Proof. Since 𝜋 −1 (∅) = ∅ and 𝜋 −1 (𝐴) = 𝑋, and both ∅ and 𝑋 are open in 𝑋, it follows that ∅ and 𝐴 are open in 𝐴. Thus T1 holds. Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of subsets of 𝐴 that are open in 𝐴. Then 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝜆 ) is open in 𝑋 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Since ( ) ⋃ ⋃ −1 𝜋 𝑈𝜆 = 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝜆 ) 𝜆∈Λ 𝜆∈Λ ⋃ is a union of open sets in 𝑋, it must be open in 𝑋. Hence, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 is open in 𝐴. Thus T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is also an element of the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋. Let 𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 be subsets of 𝐴 that are open in 𝐴. Then 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝑖 ) is open in 𝑋 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. Since ( 𝑛 ) 𝑛 ⋂ ⋂ 𝜋 −1 𝑈𝑖 = 𝜋 −1 (𝑈𝑖 ) 𝑖=1 𝑖=1

is a (finite) intersection of open sets in 𝑋, it must be open in 𝑋. Hence, 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 is open in 𝐴, and so, T3 is satisfied. Thus the quotient topology is a topology. Let  be a topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is continuous. We must show that  is coarser than the quotient topology. Since 𝜋 is continuous when 𝐴 is given  as its topology, we have for each 𝑉 ∈  that 𝜋 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑋, and so, 𝑉 is in the quotient topology. Hence,  is coarser than the quotient topology.

items.label: Quotient Map Identifying a Subset of the Real Line
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, let 𝐴 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, and let 𝜋∶ ℝ → 𝐴 be the map given by ⎧𝑎 𝑥 = 0, ⎪ 𝜋(𝑥) = ⎨𝑏 𝑥 < 0, ⎪𝑐 𝑥 > 0. ⎩ Then the quotient topology on 𝐴 induced by 𝜋 is the collection {∅, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, 𝐴} of subsets of 𝐴.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 39

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.18

items.label: Open and closed maps
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a continuous map. We say that 𝑓 is an open map if for each subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 that is open in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 . Likewise, we say that 𝑓 is a closed map if for each subset 𝑉 of 𝑋 that is closed in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑉 ) is closed in 𝑌 . If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is an open (closed) map and 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋 then it is not true in general that 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. However, if we also assume that 𝐴 is an open (closed) subset of 𝑋, then 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. Consider the case where 𝐴 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑓 is an open map. Let 𝑈 be a subset of 𝐴 that is open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑈 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 for some open subset 𝑉 of 𝑋. Then 𝑈 is also open in 𝑋, and hence, 𝑓 |𝐴 (𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is an open subset of 𝑌 . Furthermore, since 𝑓 (𝐴) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴), 𝑓 (𝑈 ) must be open in 𝑓 (𝐴).
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a continuous map. We say that 𝑓 is an open map if for each subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 that is open in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 . Likewise, we say that 𝑓 is a closed map if for each subset 𝑉 of 𝑋 that is closed in 𝑋 the image 𝑓 (𝑉 ) is closed in 𝑌 .

If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is an open (closed) map and 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑋 then it is not true in general that 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. However, if we also assume that 𝐴 is an open (closed) subset of 𝑋, then 𝑓 |𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑓 (𝐴) is an open (closed) map. Consider the case where 𝐴 is open in 𝑋 and 𝑓 is an open map. Let 𝑈 be a subset of 𝐴 that is open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑈 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 for some open subset 𝑉 of 𝑋. Then 𝑈 is also open in 𝑋, and hence, 𝑓 |𝐴 (𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is an open subset of 𝑌 . Furthermore, since 𝑓 (𝐴) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 (𝑈 ) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝐴), 𝑓 (𝑈 ) must be open in 𝑓 (𝐴).

items.label: Open and Closed Maps Need Not Be Homeomorphisms
items.statement: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed. However, the converse is, in general, not true. Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let ∗ be the space consisting of a single element. Then ℝ →∗ is both an open and a closed map but it is clearly not a homeomorphism.
items.formal_statement: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed. However, the converse is, in general, not true. Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let ∗ be the space consisting of a single element. Then ℝ →∗ is both an open and a closed map but it is clearly not a homeomorphism.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.20

items.label: Quotient Maps from Bijective Maps
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a bijective continuous map. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) 𝑓 is a homeomorphism;

(2) 𝑓 is an open map;

(3) 𝑓 is a closed map.

Proof. We will show that (1) is equivalent to (2) and that (2) is equivalent to (3). Since 𝑓 is a bijection, we have ( −1 )−1 𝑓 (𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 )

for any subset 𝑈 of 𝑋. In particular, if 𝑈 is an open subset of 𝑋 and if we assume that 𝑓 is a homeo- morphism then 𝑓 (𝑈 ) must be open in 𝑌 as 𝑓 −1 is continuous. Hence, 𝑓 is an open map. Furthermore, if we assume that 𝑓 is open instead of being a homeomorphism then 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 for 𝑈 an open subset of 𝑋. Hence, 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is continuous. Thus 𝑓 is a homeomorphism. Hence, (1) is equivalent to (2). Assume that 𝑓 is an open map. Since 𝑓 is a bijection, we have

𝑓 (𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 𝑐 ) = 𝑓 (𝑈 )𝑐 = 𝑌 ⧵ 𝑓 (𝑈 ).

Thus 𝑓 is closed: 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 is closed and 𝑌 ⧵ 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is closed. The opposite implication follows by a completely analogous argument. Hence, (2) is equivalent to (3). This completes the proof.

The following lemma establishes a relation between open and closed maps, and quotient maps.

items.label: Open or Closed Surjections Are Quotient Maps
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective contin- uous map.

(1) If 𝜋 is in addition open then it is a quotient map.

(2) If 𝜋 is in addition closed then it is a quotient map.

Proof. Assume that 𝜋 is in addition open. Let 𝑉 be a subset of 𝑌 . If 𝑉 is open in 𝑌 then 𝜋 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑋 by assumption of continuity of 𝜋. If 𝜋 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝑋 then since 𝜋 is surjective, we have

𝜋(𝜋 −1 (𝑉 )) = 𝑉

which is open in 𝑌 since we have assumed that 𝜋 is an open map. Hence, (1) holds. Now assume that 𝜋 is also closed in addition to being a surjective continuous map. Let 𝑊 be a subset of 𝑌 . If 𝑊 is closed in 𝑌 then 𝜋 −1 (𝑊 ) is closed in 𝑋 by assumption of continuity of 𝜋. If 𝜋 −1 (𝑊 ) is closed in 𝑋 then since 𝜋 is surjective, we have

𝜋(𝜋 −1 (𝑊 )) = 𝑊

which is closed in 𝑌 since we have assumed that 𝜋 is a closed map. Hence, (2) holds.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. (1) If 𝜋 is in addition open then it is a quotient map.
items.intuition: Records an auxiliary result about open or closed surjections are quotient maps that supports later statements or proofs.
