items.label: Circle as a Quotient of the Unit Interval
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Consider [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ and 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝜋(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)). Then, clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous map. We can show that 𝜋 is also closed (to do this it helps to have defined compactness). Thus by Lemma 5.22 𝜋 is a quotient map. Note that 𝜋 is not open as 𝜋([0, 𝑡)) is not open for 0 < 𝑡 < 1 (where we are using the fact that [0, 𝑡) is open in [0, 1] for 0 < 𝑡 < 1). Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] given by 𝑠 ∼ 𝑡 if and only if 𝜋(𝑠) = 𝜋(𝑡), and let

𝑝 ∶ [0, 1] → [0, 1]∕∼

be the map given by 𝑝(𝑡) = [𝑡]. Then, clearly, 𝑝 is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

𝜑 ∶ [0, 1]∕∼ → 𝑆 1

given by 𝜑([𝑡]) = 𝜋(𝑡) is then a homeomorphism from [0, 1]∕∼ with the quotient topology induced by 𝑝 to 𝑆 1 with the quotient topology induced by 𝜋. See Figure 5.6.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 41

[0] = [1] 0 1

Figure 5.6: Constructing the circle 𝑆 1 .
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Consider [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ and 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝜋(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)). Then, clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous map. We can show that 𝜋 is also closed (to do this it helps to have defined compactness). Thus by Lemma 5.22 𝜋 is a quotient map. Note that 𝜋 is not open as 𝜋([0, 𝑡)) is not open for 0 < 𝑡 < 1 (where we are using the fact that [0, 𝑡) is open in [0, 1] for 0 < 𝑡 < 1). Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] given by 𝑠 ∼ 𝑡 if and only if 𝜋(𝑠) = 𝜋(𝑡), and let 𝑝 ∶ [0, 1] → [0, 1]∕∼
items.intuition: Illustrates circle as a quotient of the unit interval in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.23

items.label: Quotient Description of a Cylinder
items.statement:
Consider [0, 1] × [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ × ℝ = ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology, and 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 × ℝ2 = ℝ4 where ℝ4 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 be the map given by

𝜋(𝑠, 𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑠), sin(2𝜋𝑠), cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)).

Clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous function. As in the previous example, we can show that 𝜋 is closed, and so, by Lemma 5.22 it is a quotient map. Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] × [0, 1] given by

(𝑠, 0) ∼ (𝑠, 1) and (0, 𝑡) ∼ (1, 𝑡),

and let 𝑝 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ be the map given by 𝑝(𝑠, 𝑡) = [𝑠, 𝑡]. Then, clearly, 𝑝 is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

𝜑 ∶ ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ → 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1

given by 𝜑([𝑠, 𝑡]) = 𝜋(𝑠, 𝑡) is then a homeomorphism from ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ with the quotient topology induced by 𝑝 to 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 with the quotient topology induced by 𝜋. The equivalence classes are then the sets

{(0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), (1, 1)}

(the four vertices of the square are identified),

{(𝑠, 0), (𝑠, 1)} and {(0, 𝑡), (1, 𝑡)}

(opposing edges of the square are identified), and

{(𝑠, 𝑡)}

for 𝑠, 𝑡 ∈ (0, 1). See Figure 5.5.

42 5.3. Quotient spaces
items.formal_statement: Consider [0, 1] × [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ × ℝ = ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology, and 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 × ℝ2 = ℝ4 where ℝ4 is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝜋(𝑠, 𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑠), sin(2𝜋𝑠), cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)).
items.intuition: Illustrates quotient description of a cylinder in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.24

items.label: Real Projective Space as a Quotient Space
items.statement:
The real projective space is the quotient space

ℝP𝑛 = 𝑆 𝑛 ∕∼

where 𝑆 𝑛 is the 𝑛-sphere and ∼ is the equivalence relation given by 𝑥 ∼ 𝑦 if and only if 𝑥 = ±𝑦, i.e., [𝑥] = {𝑥, −𝑥}. We say that 𝑥 and −𝑥 are antipodal points. The topology on ℝP𝑛 is defined by the quotient map 𝜋 ∶ 𝑆 𝑛 → ℝP𝑛 given by 𝜋(𝑥) = [𝑥].

We end this section with an alternative description of the quotient topology.
items.formal_statement: The real projective space is the quotient space ℝP𝑛 = 𝑆 𝑛 ∕∼
items.intuition: Illustrates real projective space as a quotient space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.25

items.label: Quotient Topology via Saturated Open Sets
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a surjective map. The quotient topology is the only topology on 𝐴 with the following universal property: for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous.

𝑋 𝑓 ◦𝜋 𝜋

𝐴 𝑌 𝑓

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proofs of Theorem 5.6 and Theorem 5.13 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the quotient topology  𝜋 has the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. Let 𝐴 be given the quotient topology induced by 𝜋, and assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 is continuous. By definition of the quotient topology 𝜋 −1 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑋 if and only if 𝑈 is open in 𝐴. Hence, 𝜋 is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. Now assume that 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. Let 𝑉 be an open set in 𝑌 . Since 𝜋 is a quotient map and

(𝑓 ◦𝜋)−1 (𝑉 ) = 𝜋 −1 (𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ))

is open in 𝑋 by assumption of continuity of 𝑓 ◦𝜋, it follows that 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) is open in 𝐴. Thus 𝑓 is continuous. Let  ′ be a topology on 𝐴 with the universal property that for every topological space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 , 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑓 ◦𝜋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is continuous. We must show that  ′ =  𝜋. Let  be the topology on 𝑋, and let 𝐴 be given the topology  ′ . First let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with the quotient topology induced by 𝑓 ◦𝜋. Then for 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝐴,  𝜋 ), we have

id ◦𝜋 = 𝜋 ∶ (𝑋,  ) → (𝐴,  𝜋 )

which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 43

(𝑋,  ) id ◦𝜋 𝜋

(𝐴,  ′ ) (𝐴,  𝜋 ) id

Thus any 𝑉 ∈  𝜋 must also be an element of  ′ , and so,  𝜋 ⊆  ′ . Secondly let 𝑌 = 𝐴 with  ′ as its topology. Then, clearly, 𝑓 = id ∶ (𝐴,  ′ ) → (𝐴,  ′ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that

id ◦𝜋 = 𝜋 ∶ (𝑋,  ) → (𝐴,  ′ )

is continuous. (𝑋,  ) id ◦𝜋 𝜋

(𝐴,  ′ ) (𝐴,  ′ ) id

Since the quotient topology induced by 𝜋 is the finest topology on 𝐴 such that 𝜋 is a continuous map, cf. Lemma 5.17, it follows that  ′ ⊆  𝜋 . Hence,  ′ =  𝜋 .

5.4 Exercises

Exercise 5.1 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Show that any subspace of the form (𝑎, 𝑏), i.e., an open interval, is homeomorphic to ℝ.

Exercise 5.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space and let 𝑌 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝐴 is a subset of 𝑌 , show that the subspace topology on 𝐴 inherited from 𝑌 is equal to the subspace topology on 𝐴 inherited from 𝑋.

Exercise 5.3Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider the set of rational numbers ℚ as a subspace of ℝ. Show that the subset { √ } | √ 𝐴= 𝑥∈ℚ|− 5<𝑥< 5 | of ℚ is both open and closed in ℚ.

Exercise 5.4 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑} be given the topology 𝑋 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑐, 𝑑}, {𝑐, 𝑑}, 𝑋}, and let 𝑌 = {1, 2, 3} be given the topology 𝑌 = {∅, {1}, {1, 3}, 𝑌 }. Find a basis for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑌 .

Exercise 5.5 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be subsets of 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively. Show that the topology on 𝐴 × 𝐵 as a subspace of the product 𝑋 × 𝑌 is equal to the product topology on 𝐴 × 𝐵 where 𝐴 and 𝐵 are given the subspace topology inherited from 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively.

44 5.4. Exercises

Exercise 5.6 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Show that the product topology is the coarsest topology on 𝑋 ×𝑌 for which both of the projection maps pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 ×𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 ×𝑌 → 𝑌 are continuous.

Exercise 5.7 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑋 × 𝑌 be given the product topology. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map, the subspace

𝐺 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑌 ∣ 𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥)}

of 𝑋 × 𝑌 , is homeomorphic to 𝑋.

Exercise 5.8 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Let

𝜋∶ ℝ → ℤ

be the map given by { 𝑥 𝑥∈ℤ 𝜋(𝑥) = 𝑛 𝑥 ∈ (𝑛 − 1, 𝑛 + 1), and 𝑛 is an odd integer.

Show that the quotient topology on ℤ induced by 𝜋 is equal to the digital line topology, cf. Exercise 4.6.

6. Topological properties

6.1 Connected spaces One of the fundamental results of calculus is the intermediate value theorem. The theorem says that for a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → ℝ and for a real number 𝑟 between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏) there is a real number 𝑐 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟. See Figure 6.1. From this result we can deduce that the graph of a continuous map (in this setting) is connected.

𝑦

𝑓 (𝑏)

𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥)

𝑟

𝑓 (𝑎) 𝑥 𝑎 𝑐 𝑏

Figure 6.1: The intermediate value theorem.
items.formal_statement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $A$ be a set, and let $\pi:X\to A$ be surjective. The quotient topology on $A$ is characterized by the universal property that, for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f:A\to Y$, the map $f$ is continuous if and only if $f\circ\pi:X\to Y$ is continuous.
items.intuition: This universal property is the main practical test for maps out of a quotient space: continuity can be checked after composing with the quotient map.

metadata.source: chapter:5; section:5; sectionTitle:Constructing topological spaces; number:5.26

items.conclusion: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $A$ be a set, and let $\pi:X\to A$ be surjective. The quotient topology on $A$ is characterized by the universal property that, for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f:A\to Y$, the map $f$ is continuous if and only if $f\circ\pi:X\to Y$ is continuous.

items.label: Connected space
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A separation of 𝑋 is a pair of non-empty subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 that are open in 𝑋, disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋. We say that 𝑋 is connected if there are no separations of 𝑋. Otherwise it is disconnected. The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is connected then so is 𝑌 .
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A separation of 𝑋 is a pair of non-empty subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 that are open in 𝑋, disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋. We say that 𝑋 is connected if there are no separations of 𝑋. Otherwise it is disconnected.

The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is connected then so is 𝑌 .
items.intuition: Defines connected space and fixes terminology used later in connectedness, open-sets, sets.

items.label: Connectedness in a Finite Topological Space
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}. If we equip 𝑋 with the topology 1 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is disconnected; the pair 𝑈 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and 𝑉 = {𝑑, 𝑒} is a separation of 𝑋 in this topology. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology 2 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is connected; there are no separations of 𝑋 in this topology.

46 6.1. Connected spaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}. If we equip 𝑋 with the topology 1 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is disconnected; the pair 𝑈 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and 𝑉 = {𝑑, 𝑒} is a separation of 𝑋 in this topology. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology 2 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is connected; there are no separations of 𝑋 in this topology.
items.intuition: Illustrates connectedness in a finite topological space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Indiscrete Spaces Are Connected
items.statement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is connected as there are no separations of 𝑋, i.e., there are no non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 who are disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is connected as there are no separations of 𝑋, i.e., there are no non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 who are disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋.
items.intuition: Illustrates indiscrete spaces are connected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Discrete Spaces with Multiple Points Are Disconnected
items.statement: Let 𝑋 be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝑈 = {𝑝} and 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ {𝑝}. Then the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑋.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝑈 = {𝑝} and 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ {𝑝}. Then the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑋.
items.intuition: Illustrates discrete spaces with multiple points are disconnected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: The Real Line Is Connected
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let 𝑋 = [0, 1) ∪ (1, 2) be a subspace of ℝ. Since 𝑈 = [0, 1) is open in 𝑋 (but not in ℝ) and 𝑉 = (1, 2) is open 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, they form a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.

In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that are both open and closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋. Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property that there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let 𝑋 = [0, 1) ∪ (1, 2) be a subspace of ℝ. Since 𝑈 = [0, 1) is open in 𝑋 (but not in ℝ) and 𝑉 = (1, 2) is open 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, they form a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected. In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that are both open and closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋. Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property that there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
items.intuition: Illustrates the real line is connected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Connectedness via Clopen Subsets
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that 𝑋 is disconnected if and only if there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋. Assume 𝑋 is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑈 is open in 𝑋. Since 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, we have 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 = 𝑉 . Thus 𝑈 𝑐 is open in 𝑋, and so, 𝑈 is closed in 𝑋. Hence, 𝑈 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Likewise, 𝑉 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Assume that the non-empty proper subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Let 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 . Then 𝑉 is open in 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋. Hence, the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
items.intuition: States a main result about connectedness via clopen subsets; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.

items.label: Continuous Images of Connected Spaces Are Connected
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a connected space, 𝑌 be a topological space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is connected.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a surjective continuous map and 𝑌 is disconnected then 𝑋 is disconnected. Assume that 𝑌 is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of 𝑌 . Let the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑌 . Then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) are non-empty subsets of 𝑋 which are open in 𝑋 as 𝑓 is a surjective continuous map. Furthermore,

𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ) = ∅ and 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∪ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 ) = 𝑋.

Hence, the pair 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) is a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.

A subset 𝐴 of a topological space 𝑍 is connected if 𝐴 is connected in the subspace topology. Thus the theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a connected space is connected, i.e., assuming 𝑋 is a connected space and 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map then 𝑓 (𝑋) is connected in 𝑌 .

Chapter 6. Topological properties 47

We may also describe connectedness by way of the following theorem.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a connected space, 𝑌 be a topological space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is connected.
items.intuition: States a main result about continuous images of connected spaces are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a connected space, 𝑌 be a topological space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is connected.

items.label: Connectedness via Continuous Maps to a Two-Point Discrete Space
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if every contin- uous map form 𝑋 to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that 𝑋 is disconnected if and only if there is a non-constant continuous map from 𝑋 to a discrete space (with at least two points). Assume that 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑈 be a non-empty proper subset of 𝑋 that is both open and closed in 𝑋. Let 𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} be given the discrete topology. The map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 that sends 𝑈 to 𝑎 and 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 to 𝑏 is continuous and not constant. Let 𝑌 be a discrete space with at least two points. Assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a non-constant continuous map. Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋), i.e., 𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥) for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝑓 −1 ({𝑦}) is a non-empty proper subset of 𝑋 that is both open and closed in 𝑋. Thus by Theorem 6.6, 𝑋 is disconnected.

We will prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected. To prove this we will need the following two results.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if every continuous map form 𝑋 to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.
items.intuition: States a main result about connectedness via continuous maps to a two-point discrete space; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if every continuous map form 𝑋 to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.

items.label: Separation Elements Give Distinct Images under a Map to a Two-Point Space
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a disconnected space with separation 𝑈 and 𝑉 , and let 𝐴 be a connected subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 .

Proof. Since 𝑈 and 𝑉 are open in 𝑋, the intersections 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 and 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 are both open in 𝐴 (in the subspace topology). Furthermore, the complement of 𝐴∩𝑈 in 𝐴 is equal to 𝐴∩𝑉 as 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵𝑈 = 𝑉 . Hence, 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is also closed in 𝐴. Thus by Theorem 6.6, 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is either empty or all of 𝐴 as 𝐴 is connected. If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = ∅ then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 . If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = 𝐴 then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a disconnected space with separation 𝑈 and 𝑉 , and let 𝐴 be a connected subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 .
items.intuition: Records an auxiliary result about separation elements give distinct images under a map to a two-point space that supports later statements or proofs.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a disconnected space with separation 𝑈 and 𝑉 , and let 𝐴 be a connected subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 .

items.label: Unions of Connected Subspaces with Common Point Are Connected
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let {𝐴𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of connected ⋂ ⋃ subspaces of 𝑋 such that 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is non-empty. Then 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is connected.

⋃ Proof. Let 𝑌 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 . Suppose that 𝑌 is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of 𝑌 . Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑌 . We will show that this leads to a contradiction, and so, 𝑌 must be connected. ⋂ Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 . Then either 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 or 𝑝 ∈ 𝑉 . Assume without loss of generality that 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 . By Lemma 6.9 it follows that for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ either 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑉 . Since we have assumed that 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 we must have 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑈 for all 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Thus 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 . But this implies that 𝑉 is empty, and hence, contradicts that 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑌 . Thus 𝑌 is connected.

We can prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let {𝐴𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of connected ⋂ ⋃ subspaces of 𝑋 such that 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is non-empty. Then 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is connected. ⋃
items.intuition: States a main result about unions of connected subspaces with common point are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let {𝐴𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of connected ⋂ ⋃ subspaces of 𝑋 such that 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is non-empty. Then 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is connected. ⋃

items.label: Finite Products of Connected Spaces Are Connected
items.statement:
Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be connected spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is connected.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two connected spaces. The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two connected spaces. We must prove that 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected. Since for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subspace {𝑥} × 𝑌 of 𝑋 × 𝑌 is homeomorphic to 𝑌 , it follows that {𝑥} × 𝑌 is connected. Similarly, for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace 𝑋 × {𝑦} is homeomorphic to 𝑋, and hence, 𝑋 × {𝑦} is

48 6.1. Connected spaces

connected. Thus by Theorem 6.10 it follows that for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace ({𝑥} × 𝑌 ) ∪ (𝑋 × {𝑦}) is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is ({𝑥} × 𝑌 ) ∩ (𝑋 × {𝑦}) = {(𝑥, 𝑦)} ≠ ∅. Fix 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝐴𝑦 = ({𝑥0 } × 𝑌 ) ∪ (𝑋 × {𝑦}). Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace 𝐴𝑦 of 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is equal to {(𝑥0 , 𝑦)}. Hence, ⋃ by Theorem 6.10 it follows that 𝑦∈𝑌 𝐴𝑦 is connected as it is the union of connected spaces whose intersection in non-empty. Since ⋃ 𝐴𝑦 = 𝑋 × 𝑌 , 𝑦∈𝑌

it follows that 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected.

∏ The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , if we equip the product with the ∏ product topology. If we equip 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 with the box topology the statement is no longer true.

An important example of a connected space is the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be connected spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is connected.
items.intuition: States a main result about finite products of connected spaces are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be connected spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is connected.

items.label: The real numbers are connected
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is connected.

We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.

(1) Every subset of ℝ that is bounded above has a least upper bound. This is known as the least upper bound property.

(2) If 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ ℝ with 𝑥 < 𝑦 then there is a real number 𝑧 such that 𝑥 < 𝑧 < 𝑦.

Proof. Assume that ℝ is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of ℝ. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of ℝ, and choose 𝑎 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑏 ∈ 𝑉 . We may assume without loss of generality that 𝑎 < 𝑏. Let 𝐴 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ∩ 𝑈 and 𝐵 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ∩ 𝑉 . Then the pair 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏], with 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵. Also note that 𝐴 is bounded above by 𝑏. Hence, by the least upper bound property 𝐴 has a least upper bound; 𝑐 = sup 𝐴. Thus 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑐 ⩽ 𝑏. We will show that 𝑐 belongs neither to 𝐴 nor to 𝐵 thus contradicting the fact that 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. Assume that 𝑐 ∈ 𝐵. Since 𝑎 ∉ 𝐵 and 𝐵 is open in [𝑎, 𝑏], it follows that there is a real number 𝑑 such that 𝑎 < 𝑑 < 𝑐 and (𝑑, 𝑐] ⊆ 𝐵. This implies that 𝑑 is an upper bound of 𝐴 and that 𝑑 is less than the least upper bound 𝑐. That is a contradiction, and so, 𝑐 ∉ 𝐵. Now assume that 𝑐 ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is open in [𝑎, 𝑏] and 𝑏 ∉ 𝐴, there is a real number 𝑑 such that [𝑐, 𝑑) ⊆ 𝐴. For any 𝑒 ∈ (𝑐, 𝑑) it follows that 𝑒 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑒 > 𝑐. That is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑐 is an upper bound of 𝐴. Thus 𝑐 ∉ 𝐴. Hence, 𝑐 ∉ 𝐴 and 𝑐 ∉ 𝐵. This is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑐 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] and that 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. Thus ℝ must be connected.

As an immediate consequence of Theorem 6.12, we get that open intervals of the form (𝑎, 𝑏), (−∞, 𝑏) and (𝑎, ∞) are all connected as they are all homeomorphic to ℝ (with the standard topology). We can also show that every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] is connected. Furthermore, by Theorem 6.11 and Theorem 6.12 ℝ𝑛 is a connected space.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 49
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is connected. We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.
items.intuition: States a main result about the real numbers are connected; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is connected. We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.

items.label: Generalized intermediate value theorem
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a connected space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. If 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑋 and if 𝑟 is a real number that lies between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏), there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.

Proof. Assume that 𝑟 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋). We will show that this contradicts the assumption that 𝑋 is connected. By the assumption that 𝑟 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋), i.e., 𝑓 (𝑋) ⊆ ℝ ⧵ {𝑟} = (−∞, 𝑟) ∪ (𝑟, ∞), we have a separation of 𝑋: 𝑈 = 𝑓 −1 ((−∞, 𝑟)) and 𝑉 = 𝑓 −1 ((𝑟, ∞))

are disjoint non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 whose union equals 𝑋. Thus they are a separation of 𝑋. This contradicts the assumption that 𝑋 is connected. Hence, 𝑟 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). In other words, there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.

We end this section with a discussion of path-connectivity.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a connected space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. If 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑋 and if 𝑟 is a real number that lies between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏), there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.
items.intuition: States a main result about generalized intermediate value theorem; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a connected space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. If 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑋 and if 𝑟 is a real number that lies between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏), there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.

items.label: Path-connected space
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋. A path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 is a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦 where [𝑎, 𝑏] is a subspace of ℝ with the standard topology. We say that 𝑋 is path-connected if every pair of points of 𝑋 can be joined by a path in 𝑋. The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the elements of 𝑋 and its open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is path connected then so is 𝑌 .
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋. A path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 is a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦 where [𝑎, 𝑏] is a subspace of ℝ with the standard topology. We say that 𝑋 is path-connected if every pair of points of 𝑋 can be joined by a path in 𝑋.

The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the el- ements of 𝑋 and its open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is path connected then so is 𝑌 .
items.intuition: Defines path-connected space and fixes terminology used later in connectedness, continuity, maps.

items.label: Path Connectedness of the Real Line
items.statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is path-connected as for any two points, 𝑝, 𝑞 ∈ ℝ, there is a path from 𝑝 to 𝑞 in ℝ, e.g., the path given by 𝑓 (𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑝 + 𝑡𝑞 where 𝑡 ∈ [0, 1].
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ is path-connected as for any two points, 𝑝, 𝑞 ∈ ℝ, there is a path from 𝑝 to 𝑞 in ℝ, e.g., the path given by 𝑓 (𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑝 + 𝑡𝑞 where 𝑡 ∈ [0, 1].
items.intuition: Illustrates path connectedness of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Euclidean Spaces Are Path Connected
items.statement:
For all 𝑛 ⩾ 2, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is ℝ𝑛 ⧵ {𝑝} for each 𝑝 ∈ ℝ𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 1, ℝ ⧵ {𝑝} is neither connected nor path-connected.

The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not true in general, it is true that open subsets of ℝ𝑛 that are connected (where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.
items.formal_statement: For all 𝑛 ⩾ 2, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is ℝ𝑛 ⧵ {𝑝} for each 𝑝 ∈ ℝ𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 1, ℝ ⧵ {𝑝} is neither connected nor path-connected. The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not true in general, it is true that open subsets of ℝ𝑛 that are connected (where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.
items.intuition: Illustrates euclidean spaces are path connected in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Path-connectedness implies connectedness
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. Then 𝑋 is connected.

Proof. Assume that 𝑋 is path-connected but that 𝑋 is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 to 𝑦 ∈ 𝑉 . Then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) are disjoint non-empty open subsets of [𝑎, 𝑏] whose union is equal to [𝑎, 𝑏]. Thus they are a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. This is a contradiction to the fact that [𝑎, 𝑏] is a connected space.

50 6.2. Hausdorff spaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. Then 𝑋 is connected.
items.intuition: States a main result about path-connectedness implies connectedness; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. Then 𝑋 is connected.

items.label: The topologist’s sine curve
items.statement:
The topologist’s sine curve is the subspace {( ( )) | } 1 | 0 < 𝑥 ⩽ 1 ∪ {(0, 𝑦) ∣ −1 ⩽ 𝑦 ⩽ 1} 𝑆= 𝑥, sin | 𝑥 | of ℝ2 with the standard topology. It can be shown that 𝑆 is connected but not path-connected. For a proof of this fact, see [4, pp. 156–157]. See Figure 6.2.

𝑦 𝑆

𝑥

−1

Figure 6.2: The topologist’s sine curve.

6.2 Hausdorff spaces A common feature that we typically want a topological space to have is the ability to separate the individual points. This is commonly referred to as separation axioms. We will in this section focus on the most common separation axiom.
items.formal_statement: The topologist’s sine curve is the subspace {( ( )) | } 1 | 0 < 𝑥 ⩽ 1 ∪ {(0, 𝑦) ∣ −1 ⩽ 𝑦 ⩽ 1} 𝑆= 𝑥, sin | 𝑥 | of ℝ2 with the standard topology. It can be shown that 𝑆 is connected but not path-connected. For a proof of this fact, see [4, pp. 156–157]. See Figure 6.2. 𝑦 𝑆
items.intuition: Illustrates the topologist’s sine curve in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Hausdorff Spaces
items.formal_statement: A topological space $X$ is Hausdorff if for every pair of distinct points $x,y\in X$ there exist disjoint neighborhoods $U$ of $x$ and $V$ of $y$.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. We say that 𝑋 is Hausdorff if for each pair of points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, with 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦, there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. In other words, for each pair of distinct points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 there are open subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑋 with 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑦 ∈ 𝑉 where 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅.

The property to be a Hausdorff space is completely described using the elements of 𝑋 and its open subsets, and so, it is a topological property. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is Hausdorff then so is 𝑌 . To see this let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space and let 𝑦1 and 𝑦2 be distinct points in 𝑌 . If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism then 𝑓 −1 (𝑦1 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑦2 ) are distinct points in 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is Hausdorff this means that there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈1 and 𝑈2 of 𝑓 −1 (𝑦1 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑦2 ), respectively. Thus 𝑓 (𝑈1 ) and 𝑓 (𝑈2 ) are disjoint neighborhoods of 𝑦1 and 𝑦2 , respectively. Hence, 𝑌 is Hausdorff.
items.intuition: Hausdorff spaces are spaces in which distinct points can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.

items.label: Finite Discrete Spaces Are Hausdorff
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. If we equip 𝑋 with the discrete topology then 𝑋 is Hausdorff since for all pairs of distinct points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, the open subsets {𝑥} and {𝑦} are neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, and {𝑥} ∩ {𝑦} = ∅. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, 𝑋} it is not Hausdorff; the only neighborhood of 𝑐 is 𝑈 = 𝑋, and no neighborhood 𝑉 of either 𝑎 or 𝑏 can be disjoint from 𝑋.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 51
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. If we equip 𝑋 with the discrete topology then 𝑋 is Hausdorff since for all pairs of distinct points 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋, the open subsets {𝑥} and {𝑦} are neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, and {𝑥} ∩ {𝑦} = ∅. However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, 𝑋} it is not Hausdorff; the only neighborhood of 𝑐 is 𝑈 = 𝑋, and no neighborhood 𝑉 of either 𝑎 or 𝑏 can be disjoint from 𝑋.
items.intuition: Illustrates finite discrete spaces are hausdorff in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Hausdorffness and Finer Topologies
items.statement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. If 2 is finer than 1 and 𝑋 equipped with 1 as its topology is Hausdorff then 𝑋 equipped with 2 as its topology is also Hausdorff.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1 and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. If 2 is finer than 1 and 𝑋 equipped with 1 as its topology is Hausdorff then 𝑋 equipped with 2 as its topology is also Hausdorff.
items.intuition: Illustrates hausdorffness and finer topologies in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Metric Spaces Are Hausdorff
items.statement:
Every metric space is Hausdorff.

Proof. Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝑥 and 𝑦 be two distinct points in 𝑋. We must show that there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. Let 𝛿 = 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦). Then 𝛿 > 0. Let 𝑈 = 𝖡(𝑥; 𝛿∕2) and 𝑉 = 𝖡(𝑦; 𝛿∕2) be neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. Then by M3, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅.
items.formal_statement: Every metric space is Hausdorff.
items.intuition: States a main result about metric spaces are hausdorff; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Every metric space is Hausdorff.

items.label: Euclidean Spaces Are Hausdorff
items.statement:
For all integers 𝑛 ⩾ 1, the metric space (ℝ𝑛 , 𝑑) is Hausdorff. In particular, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is Hausdorff.

All Hausdorff spaces share the property that finite subsets are closed which is an immediate con- sequence of the following theorem.
items.formal_statement: For all integers 𝑛 ⩾ 1, the metric space (ℝ𝑛 , 𝑑) is Hausdorff. In particular, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is Hausdorff. All Hausdorff spaces share the property that finite subsets are closed which is an immediate consequence of
items.intuition: Illustrates euclidean spaces are hausdorff in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Singletons Are Closed in Hausdorff Spaces
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space. Then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subset {𝑥} of 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.

Proof. Let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋 with 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦. Since 𝑋 is Hausdorff, we have neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, such that 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Then 𝑥 ∉ 𝑉 . In other words, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑉 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑉 . Since 𝑉 is open, 𝑉 𝑐 is closed in 𝑋. Thus {𝑥} ⊆ 𝑉 𝑐 , and hence, 𝑦 ∉ {𝑥}. Hence, {𝑥} = {𝑥}. Thus {𝑥} is closed in 𝑋.

There are examples of topological spaces who are not Hausdorff but have the property that finite subsets are closed. One such example is the set of real numbers equipped with the cofinite topology. We have seen that the (finite) product of connected spaces is connected, cf. Theorem 6.11. The same statement holds for Hausdorff spaces, i.e., the (finite) product of Hausdorff spaces is Hausdorff.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space. Then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subset {𝑥} of 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.
items.intuition: States a main result about singletons are closed in hausdorff spaces; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space. Then for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subset {𝑥} of 𝑋 is closed in 𝑋.

items.label: Finite Products of Hausdorff Spaces Are Hausdorff
items.statement:
Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is Hausdorff.

Proof. We prove the statement for two Hausdorff spaces. The general result then follows from an induction argument. Let (𝑥1 , 𝑦1 ) and (𝑥2 , 𝑦2 ) be two distinct points in 𝑋 × 𝑌 , i.e., 𝑥1 ≠ 𝑥2 or 𝑦1 ≠ 𝑦2 . If 𝑥1 ≠ 𝑥2 there must be neighborhoods 𝑈𝑋 and 𝑉𝑋 in 𝑋 of 𝑥1 and 𝑥2 , respectively, such that 𝑈𝑋 ∩ 𝑉𝑋 = ∅ as 𝑋 is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then 𝑈𝑋 × 𝑌 and 𝑉𝑋 × 𝑌 are neighborhoods of (𝑥1 , 𝑦1 ) and (𝑥2 , 𝑦2 ), respectively, where (𝑈𝑋 ×𝑌 )∩(𝑉𝑋 ×𝑌 ) = ∅. Similarly, if 𝑦1 ≠ 𝑦2 there must be neighborhoods 𝑈𝑌 and 𝑉𝑌 in 𝑌 of 𝑦1 and 𝑦2 , respectively, such that 𝑈𝑌 ∩𝑉𝑌 = ∅ as 𝑌 is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then 𝑋 ×𝑈𝑌 and 𝑋 × 𝑉𝑌 are neighborhoods of (𝑥1 , 𝑦1 ) and (𝑥2 , 𝑦2 ), respectively, where (𝑋 × 𝑈𝑌 ) ∩ (𝑋 × 𝑉𝑌 ) = ∅. Hence, 𝑋 × 𝑌 is Hausdorff.

∏ The theorem be extended to hold for arbitrary products, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , if we equip the product with either the product topology or the box topology.

52 6.3. Compact spaces

We end this section with a result that helps us decide whether or not a topological space is Haus- dorff.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is Hausdorff.
items.intuition: States a main result about finite products of hausdorff spaces are hausdorff; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is Hausdorff.

items.label: Hausdorff Spaces via the Diagonal
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal Δ = {(𝑥, 𝑥) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋.

Proof. Assume that 𝑋 is a Hausdorff space. Then for any two distinct points 𝑥 and 𝑦 in 𝑋, i.e., (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ Δ𝑐 = (𝑋 × 𝑋) ⧵ Δ, there are disjoint neighborhoods 𝑈 and 𝑉 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively. Thus 𝑈 × 𝑉 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑋 and (𝑈 × 𝑉 ) ∩ Δ = ∅, i.e., 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ Δ𝑐 . Hence, by Theorem 3.10, it follows that Δ𝑐 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑋, and so, Δ is closed in 𝑋 × 𝑋. Now assume that Δ is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋. Then for any point (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑋 × 𝑋 with 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦, i.e., (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ Δ𝑐 , there is a basis element 𝑈 × 𝑉 for the product topology on 𝑋 × 𝑋 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ Δ𝑐 . Since 𝑈 × 𝑉 ⊆ Δ𝑐 , we have 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Thus 𝑈 and 𝑉 are neighborhoods of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, such that 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Hence, 𝑋 is Hausdorff.

6.3 Compact spaces In Section 6.1 we saw how the intermediate value theorem might be generalized to connected spaces. In this section we will see how the extreme value theorem may be generalized to compact spaces. The extreme value theorem says that for a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → ℝ there are points 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏]. See Figure 6.3.

𝑦

𝑓 (𝑀)

𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥)

𝑓 (𝑚) 𝑥 𝑎 𝑀 𝑚 𝑏

Figure 6.3: The extreme value theorem.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal Δ = {(𝑥, 𝑥) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋.
items.intuition: States a main result about hausdorff spaces via the diagonal; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal Δ = {(𝑥, 𝑥) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋} is closed in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑋.

items.label: Cover of a space
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a collection ⋃ of subsets of 𝑋. We say that  is a cover of 𝑋, or covering of 𝑋 if 𝑋 = 𝐴∈ 𝐴. If 𝐴 is also open in 𝑋 for each 𝐴 ∈ , we say that  is an open cover of 𝑋, or open covering of 𝑋. We say that ′ is a subcover of  if ′ is another cover of 𝑋 that satisfies ′ ⊆ .
items.definition: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a collection ⋃ of subsets of 𝑋. We say that  is a cover of 𝑋, or covering of 𝑋 if 𝑋 = 𝐴∈ 𝐴. If 𝐴 is also open in 𝑋 for each 𝐴 ∈ , we say that  is an open cover of 𝑋, or open covering of 𝑋. We say that ′ is a subcover of  if ′ is another cover of 𝑋 that satisfies ′ ⊆ .
items.intuition: Defines cover of a space and fixes terminology used later in covering, open-sets, sets.

items.label: Hausdorffness Tested on Basis Elements
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. Then  is an open cover of 𝑋. Similarly, if  is a subbasis for the topology on 𝑋, then  is an open cover of 𝑋.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 53
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. Then  is an open cover of 𝑋. Similarly, if  is a subbasis for the topology on 𝑋, then  is an open cover of 𝑋.
items.intuition: Illustrates hausdorffness tested on basis elements in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Compact spaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. We say that 𝑋 is compact if every open cover  of 𝑋 contains a finite subcover. The property of being compact is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of the collection of open sets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is compact then so is 𝑌 .
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. We say that 𝑋 is compact if every open cover  of 𝑋 contains a finite subcover.

The property of being compact is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of the collection of open sets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is compact then so is 𝑌 .
items.intuition: Defines compact spaces and fixes terminology used later in compactness, covering, homeomorphism.

items.label: Finite Spaces Are Compact
items.statement: Let 𝑋 be a finite topological space. Then 𝑋 is compact as there are only finitely many different open subsets 𝐴 of 𝑋, and so, any collection covering 𝑋 must necessarily be finite.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a finite topological space. Then 𝑋 is compact as there are only finitely many different open subsets 𝐴 of 𝑋, and so, any collection covering 𝑋 must necessarily be finite.
items.intuition: Illustrates finite spaces are compact in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Indiscrete Spaces Are Compact
items.statement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is compact as the only open covers are the collections {𝑋} and {∅, 𝑋} which are finite.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is compact as the only open covers are the collections {𝑋} and {∅, 𝑋} which are finite.
items.intuition: Illustrates indiscrete spaces are compact in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Noncompactness of the Real Line
items.statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Since the open cover  = {(𝑛 − 1, 𝑛 + 1) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ}, does not admit a finite subcover, ℝ is not compact.
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Since the open cover  = {(𝑛 − 1, 𝑛 + 1) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ}, does not admit a finite subcover, ℝ is not compact.
items.intuition: Illustrates noncompactness of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Compact subspaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is compact if 𝐴 is compact in the subspace topology. If 𝐴 is a subspace of 𝑋, a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 is a cover of 𝐴 if the union of elements of  contains 𝐴.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is compact if 𝐴 is compact in the subspace topology.

If 𝐴 is a subspace of 𝑋, a collection  of subsets of 𝑋 is a cover of 𝐴 if the union of elements of  contains 𝐴.
items.intuition: Defines compact subspaces and fixes terminology used later in compactness, covering, sets.

items.label: Compactness of Subspaces via Open Covers
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact if and only if every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴.

Proof. Assume that 𝐴 is compact. Let  be a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋. Then the collection

 ′ = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 ∈ }

is an open cover of 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is compact there must be a finite subcover {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈1 , 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈2 , … , 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝑛 } of  ′ . Hence, {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } is a finite subcollection of  that covers 𝐴. Now assume that every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴. Let  = {𝑉𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝐴. Hence, by definition of the subspace topology, cf. Definition 5.1, we have for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ that 𝑉𝜆 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈𝜆 where 𝑈𝜆 is an open subset of 𝑋. Thus the collection  ′ = {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ is a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋. Then, by assumption, there must be a finite subcollection {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 } that covers 𝐴. Hence, {𝑉𝜆1 , 𝑉𝜆2 , … , 𝑉𝜆𝑛 } is a finite subcover of . Thus every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝐴 has a finite subcover, and so, 𝐴 is compact.

The following two theorems indicates that being compact and being closed are closely related properties.

54 6.3. Compact spaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact if and only if every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴.
items.intuition: Records an auxiliary result about compactness of subspaces via open covers that supports later statements or proofs.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact if and only if every cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of 𝑋 contains a finite subcollection that covers 𝐴.

items.label: Closed Subspaces of Compact Spaces Are Compact
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝐴 be a closed subset of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact.

⋃ Proof. Let  be a cover of 𝐴, i.e., 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐶∈ 𝐶, by open subsets of 𝑋. Since 𝐴 is closed in 𝑋, 𝐴𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐴 is open in 𝑋. Thus  =  ∪ {𝐴𝑐 } is an open cover of 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is compact there must be a finite subcover ′ ⊆  of 𝑋. If ′ contains 𝐴𝑐 , let ′′ = ′ ⧵ {𝐴𝑐 }. Then ′′ is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. If ′ does not contain 𝐴𝑐 then ′ is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. Either way there is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. Thus 𝐴 is compact.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝐴 be a closed subset of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact. ⋃
items.intuition: States a main result about closed subspaces of compact spaces are compact; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝐴 be a closed subset of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 is compact. ⋃

items.label: Compact Subsets of Hausdorff Spaces Are Closed
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐾 be a subset of 𝑋 which is compact. Then 𝐾 is closed in 𝑋.

Proof. We show that 𝐾 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐾 is open in 𝑋. Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐾 𝑐 . Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝐾 there are neighborhoods 𝑈𝑦 and 𝑉𝑦 of 𝑥 and 𝑦, respectively, such that 𝑈𝑦 ∩ 𝑉𝑦 = ∅, since 𝑋 is assumed to be Hausdorff and 𝑥 ≠ 𝑦. The collection  = {𝑉𝑦 ∣ 𝑦 ∈ 𝐾} of open subsets of 𝑋 covers 𝐾 since ⋃ 𝐾⊆ 𝑉𝑦 . 𝑦∈𝐾

Since 𝐾 is assumed to be compact there must be a finite subcollection {𝑉𝑦1 , 𝑉𝑦2 , … , 𝑉𝑦𝑛 } that covers 𝐾. Let 𝑉 = 𝑉𝑦1 ∪ 𝑉𝑦2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ 𝑉𝑦𝑛 , and let 𝑈 = 𝑈𝑦1 ∩ 𝑈𝑦2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑦𝑛 . Then 𝑈 is open in 𝑋, 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅. Furthermore, 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑋 ⧵ 𝐾 = 𝐾 𝑐 . Hence, 𝐾 𝑐 is open in 𝑋.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐾 be a subset of 𝑋 which is compact. Then 𝐾 is closed in 𝑋.
items.intuition: States a main result about compact subsets of hausdorff spaces are closed; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐾 be a subset of 𝑋 which is compact. Then 𝐾 is closed in 𝑋.

items.label: Continuous Images of Compact Spaces Are Compact
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a compact space, 𝑌 a topological space and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is compact.

Proof. Let  = {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be an open cover of 𝑌 . Then  = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆 )}𝜆∈Λ is an open cover of 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is compact there must be a finite subcover ′ = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆1 ), 𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆2 ), … , 𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝜆𝑛 )} of . Then  ′ = {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 } is a finite subcover of . Hence, 𝑌 is compact.

The theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a compact space is compact, i.e., assuming 𝑋 is a compact space and 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map then 𝑓 (𝑋) is compact.

We will prove that the (finite) product of compact spaces is compact. To prove this we need the following result.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, 𝑌 a topological space and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is compact.
items.intuition: States a main result about continuous images of compact spaces are compact; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, 𝑌 a topological space and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is compact.

items.label: Tube lemma
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑌 be a compact space. If 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑈 is an open set in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑌 containing {𝑥} × 𝑌 , then there is a neighborhood 𝑊 of 𝑥 in 𝑋 such that 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 .

The set 𝑊 × 𝑌 is often called a tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 . See Figure 6.4.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 55

𝑌 𝑋×𝑌 𝑈

𝑊 𝑥 𝑋

Figure 6.4: A tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 .

Proof. As 𝑈 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑌 and (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ {𝑥} × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 for all 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 , there is a basis element 𝑊𝑦 ×𝑉𝑦 ⊆ 𝑈 for the product topology on 𝑋 ×𝑌 such that (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ 𝑊𝑦 ×𝑉𝑦 . The collection {𝑉𝑦 }𝑦∈𝑌 is an open cover of 𝑌 . Since 𝑌 is compact there must be a finite subcover of {𝑉𝑦 }𝑦∈𝑌 , say, {𝑉𝑦1 , 𝑉𝑦2 , … , 𝑉𝑦𝑛 }. Let 𝑛 ⋂ 𝑊 = 𝑊𝑦𝑖 . 𝑖=1

Then 𝑊 is open in 𝑋, and it must contain 𝑥. Clearly, 𝑛 ( ) ⋃ 𝑊 ×𝑌 ⊆ 𝑊𝑦𝑖 × 𝑉𝑦𝑖 ⊆ 𝑈 . 𝑖=1

Thus {𝑥} × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑊 × 𝑌 and 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 .

The lemma is not true if we remove the assumption that 𝑌 is compact; the open set

𝑈 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ × ℝ ∣ |𝑥𝑦| < 1} ⊆ ℝ × ℝ

does contain {0} × ℝ but does not contain any tube 𝑊 × ℝ containing {0} × ℝ. (Here we have assumed that ℝ is given the standard topology, and hence, it is not compact, cf. Example 6.32.)
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑌 be a compact space. If 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑈 is an open set in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑌 containing {𝑥} × 𝑌 , then there is a neighborhood 𝑊 of 𝑥 in 𝑋 such that 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 . The set 𝑊 × 𝑌 is often called a tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 . See Figure 6.4.
items.intuition: Records an auxiliary result about tube lemma that supports later statements or proofs.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑌 be a compact space. If 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and 𝑈 is an open set in the product space 𝑋 × 𝑌 containing {𝑥} × 𝑌 , then there is a neighborhood 𝑊 of 𝑥 in 𝑋 such that 𝑊 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 . The set 𝑊 × 𝑌 is often called a tube about {𝑥} × 𝑌 . See Figure 6.4.

items.label: Finite Products of Compact Spaces Are Compact
items.statement:
Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be compact spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is compact.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two compact spaces. The general result then follows by an inductive argument. Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be compact spaces. Let  be an open cover of 𝑋 × 𝑌 . We must show that there is a finite subcover ′ of . For each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, {𝑥} × 𝑌 is compact as it is homeomorphic to 𝑌 which is assumed to be compact. Thus there is a finite subcollection 𝑥 of  that covers {𝑥} × 𝑌 . Let ⋃ 𝑈𝑥 = 𝐴𝑥 ∈𝑥 𝐴𝑥 . Then 𝑈𝑥 is open in 𝑋 × 𝑌 and contains {𝑥} × 𝑌 . Thus by Lemma 6.38 for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a neighborhood 𝑊𝑥 ⊆ 𝑋 such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑊𝑥 and 𝑊𝑥 × 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈𝑥 . Furthermore, 𝑥 covers 𝑊𝑥 × 𝑌 . Now let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 vary. The collection {𝑊𝑥 }𝑥∈𝑋 is then an open cover of 𝑋. Since 𝑋 is compact, there must be a finite subcover {𝑊𝑥1 , 𝑊𝑥2 , … , 𝑊𝑥𝑛 } of {𝑊𝑥 }𝑥∈𝑋 . For each 1 ⩽ 𝑖 ⩽ 𝑛 the subspace

56 6.3. Compact spaces

𝑊𝑥𝑖 × 𝑌 is covered by the finite subcollection 𝑥𝑖 of . Hence,

𝑛 ⋃ 𝑋×𝑌 = 𝑊𝑥 𝑖 × 𝑌 𝑖=1 ⋃𝑛 is covered by the subcollection ′ = 𝑖=1 𝑥𝑖 of . Thus 𝑋 × 𝑌 is compact.

The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products of compact spaces if we equip the product with the product topology. This is known as Tychonoff’s theorem. It is a deep result whose proof uses several original ideas. If we equip the product with the box topology the statement is no longer true. See, e.g., [4, §37] for a discussion and proof of Tychonoff’s theorem.

We have already seen that the real line (with the standard topology) is not compact, cf. Exam- ple 6.32. The next theorem states that all closed intervals of the real line are compact.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be compact spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is compact.
items.intuition: States a main result about finite products of compact spaces are compact; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be compact spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 × ⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is compact.

items.label: Compact Subsets of the Real Line
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] in ℝ is compact.

Proof. Let  = {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a cover of [𝑎, 𝑏] by open subsets of ℝ, and let

𝑆 = {𝑥 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] ∣ [𝑎, 𝑥] is covered by a finite subcollection of }.

Note that 𝑆 is bounded above by 𝑏. Since 𝑎 ∈ 𝑈𝜆 for some 𝜆 ∈ Λ, the singleton {𝑈𝜆 } is a finite subcollection of  that covers [𝑎, 𝑎] = {𝑎}. Hence, 𝑆 is non-empty and it is bounded above. Thus 𝑆 has a least upper bound; 𝑐 = sup 𝑆. Clearly, 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑐 ⩽ 𝑏. We will show that 𝑐 ∈ 𝑆. The result follows if we can also show that 𝑐 = 𝑏. Choose 𝜆′ ∈ Λ with 𝑐 ∈ 𝑈𝜆′ . Since 𝑈𝜆′ is open in ℝ there is a real number 𝜖 > 0 such that (𝑐 − 𝜖, 𝑐 + 𝜖) ⊆ 𝑈𝜆′ . Moreover, there is an 𝑥 ∈ 𝑆 such that 𝑐 − 𝜖 < 𝑥, and so, by definition of 𝑆 there is a finite subcollection ⋃ {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 } of  such that [𝑎, 𝑥] ⊆ 𝑛𝑖=1 𝑈𝜆𝑖 . Furthermore, [𝑥, 𝑐] ⊆ 𝑈𝜆′ . Thus [𝑎, 𝑐] = [𝑎, 𝑥] ∪ [𝑥, 𝑐] is covered by the finite subcollection {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 , 𝑈𝜆′ } of . Hence, 𝑐 ∈ 𝑆. We now show that 𝑐 = 𝑏. Assume that 𝑐 < 𝑏. Then there must be a 𝑦 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑐 < 𝑦 < 𝑐 + 𝜖. Thus [𝑎, 𝑦] is covered by the subcollection {𝑈𝜆1 , 𝑈𝜆2 , … , 𝑈𝜆𝑛 , 𝑈𝜆′ } of  such that 𝑦 ∈ 𝑆. This is a contradiction of the fact that 𝑐 is an upper bound. Hence, 𝑐 = 𝑏.

In order to state and prove the Heine–Borel theorem we need the following definition.
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] in ℝ is compact.
items.intuition: States a main result about compact subsets of the real line; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] in ℝ is compact.

items.label: Bounded subsets
items.formal_statement: Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is bounded if there is an 𝑀 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) ⩽ 𝑀 for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴. Equivalently, we may say that a subset 𝐴 of a metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is bounded if there is a 𝐾 ∈ ℝ and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑑(𝑎, 𝑥) ⩽ 𝐾 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. In particular, this means that a subset of ℝ𝑛 equipped with the Euclidean metric is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius centered at the origin.
items.definition:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. We say that 𝐴 is bounded if there is an 𝑀 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) ⩽ 𝑀 for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴.

Equivalently, we may say that a subset 𝐴 of a metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is bounded if there is a 𝐾 ∈ ℝ and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑑(𝑎, 𝑥) ⩽ 𝐾 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. In particular, this means that a subset of ℝ𝑛 equipped with the Euclidean metric is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius centered at the origin.
items.intuition: Defines bounded subsets and fixes terminology used later in metric, sets.

items.label: Heine–Borel
items.statement:
Let ℝ𝑛 be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Eu- clidean metric. A subset 𝐴 of ℝ𝑛 is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 57

Proof. Assume that 𝐴 is compact. By Theorem 6.22, ℝ𝑛 is Hausdorff, and so, by Theorem 6.36 𝐴 is closed in ℝ𝑛 . We must show that 𝐴 is also bounded. Let  = {𝖡(0; 𝑛) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ+ }, i.e., a collection of open balls centered at the origin in ℝ𝑛 . Then  is a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of ℝ𝑛 . Since 𝐴 is compact there must be a finite subcollection of  that covers 𝐴. Thus there is an 𝑁 ∈ ℤ+ such that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝖡(0; 𝑁). Hence, for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴 we have 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) < 2𝑁. Thus 𝐴 is bounded. Now assume that 𝐴 is bounded and closed in ℝ𝑛 . Let 𝑝 = (𝑝1 , 𝑝2 , … , 𝑝𝑛 ) ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is bounded there is an 𝑀 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑑(𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ) ⩽ 𝑀 for all 𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴. Then 𝐴 is contained in the product space 𝑃 = [𝑝1 − 𝑀, 𝑝1 + 𝑀] × [𝑝2 − 𝑀, 𝑝2 + 𝑀] × ⋯ × [𝑝𝑛 − 𝑀, 𝑝𝑛 + 𝑀]

which by Theorem 6.40 and Theorem 6.39 is compact. Since 𝐴 is closed in ℝ𝑛 and a subset of 𝑃 , it follows by extending Theorem 6.35 to our setting that 𝐴 is compact. Specifically, let  be a cover of 𝐴 by open subsets of ℝ𝑛 . Since 𝐴 is closed in ℝ𝑛 , 𝐴𝑐 = ℝ𝑛 ⧵ 𝐴 is open in ℝ𝑛 . Thus

 =  ∪ {𝐴𝑐 }

is an open cover of ℝ𝑛 , and thus it is also an open cover of 𝑃 in the subspace topology. Since 𝑃 is compact there must be a finite subcover of  that covers 𝑃 . This implies that there is a finite subcover of  that covers 𝐴. Thus 𝐴 is compact.

An immediate consequence of Theorem 6.42 is that 𝑆 𝑛 considered as a subspace of ℝ𝑛+1 is com- pact, and hence, that the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 is compact. We end this section with a proof of the generalized extreme value theorem.
items.formal_statement: Let ℝ𝑛 be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Euclidean metric. A subset 𝐴 of ℝ𝑛 is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
items.intuition: States a main result about heine–borel; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let ℝ𝑛 be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Euclidean metric. A subset 𝐴 of ℝ𝑛 is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

items.label: Generalized extreme value theorem
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. Then there are 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.

Proof. By Theorem 6.37, 𝑓 (𝑋) is compact. We must show that 𝑓 (𝑋) contains its supremum and its infimum. If it does, then by setting 𝑓 (𝑚) = inf 𝑓 (𝑋) and 𝑓 (𝑀) = sup 𝑓 (𝑋) the theorem follows. We prove that 𝑓 (𝑋) contains its supremum. The proof for the infimum is similar. Since 𝑓 (𝑋) is compact, it is closed and bounded by Theorem 6.42. In particular, 𝑓 (𝑋) is bounded above. Hence, the set 𝑓 (𝑋) has a least upper bound; 𝑠 = sup 𝑓 (𝑋). Thus 𝑝 ⩽ 𝑠 for all 𝑝 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). We must show that 𝑠 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). Assume that 𝑠 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋). Since 𝑓 (𝑋) is closed, i.e., 𝑓 (𝑋)𝑐 = ℝ⧵𝑓 (𝑋) is open, it follows that there is a real number 𝜖 > 0 such that (𝑠 − 𝜖, 𝑠 + 𝜖) ⊆ 𝑓 (𝑋)𝑐 , and so,

(𝑠 − 𝜖, 𝑠 + 𝜖) ∩ 𝑓 (𝑋) = ∅.

Hence, there is a real number 𝑦 such that 𝑦 is an upper bound of 𝑓 (𝑋) and 𝑠 − 𝜖 < 𝑦 < 𝑠. This is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑠 is the least upper bound of 𝑓 (𝑋). Hence, 𝑠 = sup 𝑓 (𝑋) ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋).

58 6.4. Exercises

6.4 Exercises

Exercise 6.1 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is surjective continuous map and 𝑋 is path-connected then 𝑌 is also path-connected.

We say that a subset 𝐴 of a topological space 𝑋 is path-connected if 𝐴 is path-connected in the subspace topology. Hence, the previous exercise may be extended to saying that the continuous image of a path-connected space is path-connected.

Exercise 6.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 be subspaces of 𝑋.

(a) Show that if 𝐴 is connected then so is 𝐵.

(b) Show that [𝑎, 𝑏), (𝑎, 𝑏], [𝑎, 𝑏], (−∞, 𝑏] and [𝑎, ∞) are all connected spaces when considered as subspaces of ℝ with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.3 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and consider 𝐼 = [0, 1] as a subspace of ℝ where ℝ is given the standard topology. Furthermore, let the cone on 𝑋 be quotient space 𝐶𝑋 = 𝑋×𝐼∕∼, where ∼ is the equivalence relation on the product space 𝑋 × 𝐼 given by (𝑥, 0) ∼ (𝑥′ , 0) for all 𝑥, 𝑥′ ∈ 𝑋. Show that 𝐶𝑋 is path-connected.

Exercise 6.4 Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Show that 𝐴 is Hausdorff.

Exercise 6.5 Let 𝑋 be an infinite set with the cofinite topology.

(a) Show that 𝑋 is compact.

(b) Show that any subset of 𝑋 is compact.

Exercise 6.6 Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑌 be a Hausdorff space. Furthermore, let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a continuous map.

(a) Show that 𝑓 is a closed map.

(b) Show that if 𝑓 is a surjective continuous map, then 𝑓 is a quotient map.

(c) Show that if 𝑓 a bijective continuous map, then 𝑓 is a homeomorphism.

(d) Show that 𝑓 is proper, i.e., for each subset 𝐾 of 𝑌 that is compact the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐾) is compact.

Exercise 6.7 Show that the surface of the cube centered at the origin,

𝐶 = {(𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) ∈ ℝ3 ∣ max{|𝑥|, |𝑦|, |𝑧|} = 1},

and the 2-sphere, { } |√ 𝑆 2 = (𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) ∈ ℝ3 | 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 + 𝑧2 = 1 , |

Chapter 6. Topological properties 59

are homeomorphic where they are both considered to be subspaces of ℝ3 with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.8 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴1 .𝐴2 , … , 𝐴𝑛 be subspaces of 𝑋 each of which is compact. Show that 𝑛 ⋃ 𝐴𝑖 𝑖=1

is compact.

7. The fundamental group

7.1 Homotopy of paths The topological properties we have discussed so far do not help us to distinguish between, e.g., the 2-sphere 𝑆 2 and the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 . In order to prove that 𝑆 2 and 𝑇 2 are not topologically equivalent, i.e., 𝑆 2 ≇ 𝑇 2 , we need new properties and new techniques. A such property is that of simple connectedness. A path-connected space 𝑋 is, roughly speaking, simply connected if every closed curve in 𝑋 can be shrunk to a point in 𝑋. The 2-sphere 𝑆 2 is simply connected as any closed curve on 𝑆 2 can be shrunk to a point in 𝑆 2 . On the other hand, the torus 𝑇 2 is not simply connected as there are closed curves which cannot be shrunk to a point in 𝑇 2 . See Figure 7.1. To be simply connected is a topological property. Hence, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and one of them is simply connected then so is the other. We can express that a path connected space is simply connected by saying that its fundamental group is trivial. In particular, if 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 then their fundamental groups are isomorphic. We will show that the fundamental group of 𝑆 2 is not isomorphic to the fundamental group of 𝑇 2 . Hence, 𝑆 2 ≇ 𝑇 2 . Computing fundamental groups provides us with more information than just that of being simply connected.

Figure 7.1: The 2-sphere 𝑆 2 is simply connected while the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 ×𝑆 1 is not simply connected.

In order to define the fundamental group of a topological space, we will need the concept of ho- motopy (of paths).
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. Then there are 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.
items.intuition: States a main result about generalized extreme value theorem; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
items.conclusion: Let 𝑋 be a compact space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. Then there are 𝑚, 𝑀 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑚) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑥) ⩽ 𝑓 (𝑀) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.

items.label: Homotopy
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps. Furthermore, let ℝ be the set of real numbers with the standard topology, 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ, and let 𝑋 × 𝐼 be given the product topology. We say that 𝑓0 is homotopic to 𝑓1 , written 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 , if there is a continuous map 𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑌
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps. Furthermore, let ℝ be the set of real numbers with the standard topology, 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ, and let 𝑋 × 𝐼 be given the product topology. We say that 𝑓0 is homotopic to 𝑓1 , written 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 , if there is a continuous map

𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑌

such that 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑓0 (𝑥) and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) = 𝑓1 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. The map 𝐻 is called a homotopy between 𝑓0 and 𝑓1 . If 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 and 𝑓1 is a constant map, we say that 𝑓0 is nullhomotopic.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 61

We can think of a homotopy as a continuous one-parameter family {𝐻|𝑋×{𝑡} }𝑡∈𝐼 of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . See Figure 7.2.

𝑓1 𝐼 𝑌 𝑡 𝐻

metadata.source: chapter:7; section:7; sectionTitle:The fundamental group; number:7.1

items.label: Straight-line homotopy
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then any two continu- ous maps 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 , where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology, are homotopic. The map 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → ℝ𝑛 given by

𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑓0 (𝑥) + 𝑡𝑓1 (𝑥).

for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼 = [0, 1] is a homotopy between 𝑓0 and 𝑓1 . We call it the straight-line homotopy as it deforms for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the point 𝑓0 (𝑥) to the point 𝑓1 (𝑥) along the straight-line segment between them. In particular, any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 is homotopic to the constant map 𝑐 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 given by 𝑐(𝑥) = 0 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, any continuous map into ℝ𝑛 is nullhomotopic.

We will need the following lemma to prove that ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps between a topological space 𝑋 and a topological space 𝑌 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then any two continuous maps 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 , where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology, are homotopic. The map 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → ℝ𝑛 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑓0 (𝑥) + 𝑡𝑓1 (𝑥).
items.intuition: Illustrates straight-line homotopy in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Pasting lemma
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 be a topological space, where 𝐴 and 𝐵 are closed in 𝑋. Furthermore, let 𝑌 be a topological space, and assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐵 → 𝑌 are continuous maps. If 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵, then the map ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 given by { 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ℎ(𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑥) 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵 is continuous.

Proof. We will prove that the preimage of a closed subset of 𝑌 under ℎ is closed in 𝑋. Let 𝐶 be a closed subset of 𝑌 . Then ℎ−1 (𝐶) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐶) ∪ 𝑔 −1 (𝐶). Since 𝑓 is continuous, it follows that 𝑓 −1 (𝐶) is closed in 𝐴, and so, it is closed in 𝑋. Similarly, 𝑔 −1 (𝐶) is closed in 𝐵, and so, it is closed in 𝑋. Hence, ℎ−1 (𝐶) = 𝑓 −1 (𝐶) ∪ 𝑔 −1 (𝐶)

62 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is closed in 𝑋 as it is the union of two closed subsets of 𝑋. Thus ℎ is continuous.

items.label: Homotopy Relative Endpoints Is an Equivalence Relation
items.statement:
The relation ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from a topological space 𝑋 to a topological space 𝑌 .

Proof. We must show that ≃ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. For any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Then 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑥) and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) = 𝑓 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, 𝑓 ≃ 𝑓 . Thus ≃ is reflexive. Let 𝑓0 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps, and let 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy between them. Then 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1 − 𝑡) is a homotopy between 𝑓1 and 𝑓0 . Hence, 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 implies 𝑓1 ≃ 𝑓0 . Thus ≃ is symmetric. To show that ≃ is transitive, we must show that if 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 and 𝑓1 ≃ 𝑓2 , then 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓2 . Let 𝐻1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓1 , and let 𝐻2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy from 𝑓1 to 𝑓2 . Then there is a map 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by { 𝐻1 (𝑥, 2𝑡) 0 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (𝑥, 2𝑡 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1. It is continuous for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼 = [0, 1] by Lemma 7.3 since 𝑋 × [0, 1∕2] and 𝑋 × [1∕2, 1] are closed subsets of 𝑋 × 𝐼 whose union is equal to 𝑋 × 𝐼, and 𝐻1 (𝑥, 1) = 𝐻2 (𝑥, 0) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 is a homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓2 . Thus ≃ is transitive.
items.formal_statement: The relation ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from a topological space 𝑋 to a topological space 𝑌 .
items.intuition: States a main result about homotopy relative endpoints is an equivalence relation; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.

items.label: Homotopy classes
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) be the set of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . The homotopy classes in 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) are the equivalence classes under the relation ≃. We write [𝑓 ] for the homotopy class of 𝑓 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ), i.e., [𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) ∣ 𝑓 ≃ 𝑔},
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) be the set of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . The homotopy classes in 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) are the equivalence classes under the relation ≃. We write [𝑓 ] for the homotopy class of 𝑓 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ), i.e.,

[𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) ∣ 𝑓 ≃ 𝑔},

and we write [𝑋, 𝑌 ] for the set of homotopy classes of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 , i.e., [𝑋, 𝑌 ] = 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 )∕≃.

If we can connect two points 𝑥 and 𝑦 in a topological space 𝑋 by a path, i.e., a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦, cf. Definition 6.14, we say that they lie in the same path component. Specifically, the relation ∼𝑝 given by 𝑥 ∼𝑝 𝑦 if and only if there is a path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 in 𝑋, is an equivalence relation on 𝑋. We say that an equivalence class in 𝑋 under ∼𝑝 is a path component. We write 𝜋0 (𝑋) for the set of path components of 𝑋. Note that 𝜋0 (𝑋) consists of only one element if and only if 𝑋 is path-connected.
items.intuition: Defines homotopy classes and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homotopy, maps.

metadata.source: chapter:7; section:7; sectionTitle:The fundamental group; number:7.5

items.label: Constant Paths Under Continuous Maps
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑥0 } and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the continuous maps 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 correspond to the points 𝑓 (𝑥0 ) = 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 . Two continuous maps 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 are homotopic if and only if there is a path from 𝑦0 = 𝑓0 (𝑥0 ) to 𝑦1 = 𝑓1 (𝑥0 ). Hence, the homotopy classes of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 correspond to the path components of 𝑌 , and [𝑋, 𝑌 ] corresponds to 𝜋0 (𝑌 ).

We are particularly interested in the case where we have homotopies between paths in a topological space 𝑋. For simplicity, let all paths be continuous maps from 𝐼 = [0, 1] to 𝑋.

items.label: Path homotopy
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. We say that two paths 𝑓 , 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 are path homotopic, written 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔, if there is a continuous map 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 such that 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑔(𝑠)
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. We say that two paths 𝑓 , 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 are path homotopic, written 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔, if there is a continuous map 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 such that

𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑔(𝑠)

for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and

𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1

for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We call 𝐻 a path homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑔.
items.intuition: Defines path homotopy and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homotopy, maps.

items.label: Straight-Line Homotopy of Paths in Euclidean Space
items.statement:

𝑓 (𝑠) = (cos(𝜋𝑠), sin(𝜋𝑠)) ( ) 𝑔(𝑠) = cos(𝜋𝑠), sin(𝜋𝑠)

and

ℎ(𝑠) = (cos(𝜋𝑠), − sin(𝜋𝑠))

for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. Then the straight-line homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑔 is a path homotopy but there are no path homotopies from either 𝑓 or 𝑔 to ℎ. See Figure 7.4.

items.label: Path Connectedness Is an Equivalence Relation
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. Then the relation ≃𝑝 is an equivalence relation on the set of all paths from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 in 𝑋.

Proof. The result follows by proving that the homotopies constructed in the proof of Theorem 7.4 when applied to paths are path homotopies. Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . Then the constant homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by

𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑠)

is path homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑓 : 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑓 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and 𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (0) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (1) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Assume that 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path homotopy between paths 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . Then the homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by

𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑠, 1 − 𝑡)

is a path homotopy from 𝑔 to 𝑓 : 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝑔(𝑠) and 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and 𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝐻(0, 1 − 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝐻(1, 1 − 𝑡) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Finally, assume that 𝑓0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋, 𝑓1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 and 𝑓2 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 are paths from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , and that 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓1 and that 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path homotopy from 𝑓1 to 𝑓2 . Then the homotopy 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by { 𝐻1 (𝑠, 2𝑡) 0 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (𝑠, 2𝑡 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑡 ⩽ 1

is a path homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓2 : (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 0) = 𝐻1 (𝑠, 0) = 𝑓0 (𝑠) and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 1) = 𝐻2 (𝑠, 1) = 𝑓2 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼.

items.label: Path homotopy classes
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , we write [𝑓 ] for its path homotopy class, i.e., [𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 ∣ 𝑔 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔}.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , we write [𝑓 ] for its path homotopy class, i.e.,

[𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 ∣ 𝑔 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑓 ≃𝑝 𝑔}.

The fundamental group of a topological space 𝑋 with basepoint 𝑥0 is, as a set, the set of path homotopy classes of paths from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 , i.e., the set of path homotopy classes of loops based at 𝑥0 . The group structure of the fundamental group is derived from the following product.

items.label: Product of paths
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , we define the product of 𝑓 and 𝑔 as the path 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥2 given by { 𝑓 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(𝑠) = 𝑔(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. Note that the map 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 is well-defined and continuous by Lemma 7.3. Furthermore, as (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(0) = 𝑓 (0) = 𝑥0 and (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(1) = 𝑔(1) = 𝑥2 , 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥2 . See Figure 7.5.

items.label: Associativity of Path Products up to Homotopy
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , then the product 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by

[𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] = [𝑓 ∗ 𝑔].

Proof. Let 𝑓 ′ ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , and let 𝑔 ′ ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 . Fur- thermore, let 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑓 ′ , and let 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path homotopy from 𝑔 to 𝑔 ′ . Then the map 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 given by { 𝐻1 (2𝑠, 𝑡) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (2𝑠 − 1, 𝑡) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1

66 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is a path homotopy from 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 to 𝑓 ′ ∗ 𝑔 ′ : it is well-defined since 𝐻1 (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1 = 𝐻2 (0, 𝑡) for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼 and it is continuous by Lemma 7.3, and

(𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 0) = (𝑓 ∗ 𝑔)(𝑠) and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(𝑠, 1) = (𝑓 ′ ∗ 𝑔 ′ )(𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(0, 𝑡) = 𝐻1 (0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and (𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 )(1, 𝑡) = 𝐻2 (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥2 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼.

Hence, 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] = [𝑓 ∗ 𝑔].

items.label: Path Products Have Two-Sided Homotopy Inverses
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then the product of paths, ∗, has the following properties on the set of path homotopy classes in 𝑋.

Associativity Let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 , 𝑥2 and 𝑥3 be points in 𝑋. If 𝑓0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 , and 𝑓2 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥2 to 𝑥3 , then

([𝑓0 ] ∗ [𝑓1 ]) ∗ [𝑓2 ] = [𝑓0 ] ∗ ([𝑓1 ] ∗ [𝑓2 ]).

Left and right units For 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, let 𝑐𝑥 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 denote the constant path at 𝑥, given by 𝑐𝑥 (𝑠) = 𝑥 for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 then

[𝑐𝑥0 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑐𝑥1 ].

Inverse If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be the reverse path from 𝑥1 to 𝑥0 , given by 𝑓 (𝑠) = 𝑓 (1 − 𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. Then

[𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑐𝑥0 ] and [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑐𝑥1 ].

Proof. We prove that ∗ is an associative operation on the set of path homotopy classes in 𝑋. By definition, we have { (𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 )(2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ((𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 ) ∗ 𝑓2 )(𝑠) = 𝑓2 (2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎧𝑓0 (4𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕4 ⎪ = ⎨𝑓1 (4𝑠 − 1) 1∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (7.1) ⎪𝑓 (2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1, ⎩ 2

and { 𝑓0 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ))(𝑠) = (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 )(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎧𝑓0 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪ = ⎨𝑓1 (4𝑠 − 2) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 3∕4 (7.2) ⎪𝑓 (4𝑠 − 3) 3∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. ⎩ 2

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 67

We must show that there is a path homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 from (𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 ) ∗ 𝑓2 to 𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ). If there is such a path homotopy then [(𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 ) ∗ 𝑓2 ] = [𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 )], and hence, by Lemma 7.13 ([𝑓0 ] ∗ [𝑓1 ]) ∗ [𝑓2 ] = [𝑓0 ] ∗ ([𝑓1 ] ∗ [𝑓2 ]). We can construct a path homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 from (𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 ) ∗ 𝑓2 to 𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ) as follows. Draw the straight lines connecting the domain of 𝑓1 in Equation 7.1 and the domain of 𝑓1 in Equation 7.2 in 𝐼 × 𝐼. See Figure 7.6. 1 3 2 4 𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ) 𝑓0 𝑓1 𝑓2
