We may then define 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 by ⎧𝑓0 (4𝑠∕(1 + 𝑡)) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ (1 + 𝑡)∕4 ⎪ 𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = ⎨𝑓1 (4𝑠 − 𝑡 − 1) (1 + 𝑡)∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ (2 + 𝑡)∕4 ⎪𝑓 ((4𝑠 − 𝑡 − 2)∕(2 − 𝑡)) (2 + 𝑡)∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. ⎩ 2 By Lemma 7.3, 𝐻 is continuous. Since ⎧𝑓0 (4𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕4 ⎪ 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = ⎨𝑓1 (4𝑠 − 1) 1∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪𝑓 (2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎩ 2 it follows that 𝐻(𝑠, 0) = ((𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 ) ∗ 𝑓2 )(𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼 by Equation 7.1. Similarly, since ⎧𝑓0 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪ 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = ⎨𝑓1 (4𝑠 − 2) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 3∕4 ⎪𝑓 (4𝑠 − 3) 3∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎩ 2

68 7.1. Homotopy of paths

it follows that 𝐻(𝑠, 1) = (𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ))(𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼 by Equation 7.2. Finally, since 𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝑓0 (0) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻(1, 𝑡) = 𝑓2 (1) = 𝑥3 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, it follows that 𝐻 is a path homotopy from (𝑓0 ∗ 𝑓1 ) ∗ 𝑓2 to 𝑓0 ∗ (𝑓1 ∗ 𝑓2 ). We prove that the constant path is a left unit under ∗ for path homotopy classes. The proof for the fact that the constant path is a right unit under ∗ is similar. By definition, we have { 𝑐𝑥0 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑐𝑥0 ∗ 𝑓 )(𝑠) = 𝑓 (2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 { 𝑥0 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 = (7.3) 𝑓 (2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1.

We must show that there is a path homotopy 𝐻 ′ ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑐𝑥0 ∗ 𝑓 to 𝑓 . If there is such a path homotopy then [𝑐𝑥0 ∗ 𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ], and hence, by Lemma 7.13 [𝑐𝑥0 ] ∗ [𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ]. We can construct a path homotopy 𝐻 ′ ∶ 𝐼 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑐𝑥0 ∗ 𝑓 to 𝑓 as follows. Draw the straight line from (𝑠, 𝑡) = (1∕2, 0) to (𝑠, 𝑡) = (0, 1). See Figure 7.7.

The equation for the straight line from (𝑠, 𝑡) = (1∕2, 0) to (𝑠, 𝑡) = (0, 1) is given by 1−𝑡 𝑠= . We may then define 𝐻 ′ ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 by { ′ 𝑥0 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ (1 − 𝑡)∕2 𝐻 (𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝑓 ((2𝑠 + 𝑡 − 1)∕(1 + 𝑡)) (1 − 𝑡)∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1.

it follows by Equation 7.3 that 𝐻 ′ (𝑠, 0) = (𝑐𝑥0 ∗ 𝑓 )(𝑠), and clearly, 𝐻 ′ (𝑠, 1) = 𝑓 (𝑠), for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. Finally, since 𝐻 ′ (0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻 ′ (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, it follows that 𝐻 ′ is a path homotopy from 𝑐𝑥0 ∗ 𝑓 to 𝑓 . Finally, we prove that for each path homotopy class there is an inverse path homotopy class under ∗. By definition, we have { 𝑓 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑓 ∗ 𝑓 )(𝑠) = 𝑓 (2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 { 𝑓 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 = 𝑓 (1 − (2𝑠 − 1)) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 { 𝑓 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 = (7.4) 𝑓 (2 − 2𝑠) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1.

Hence, 𝑓 ∗ 𝑓 is a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 . We must show that there is a path homotopy 𝐻 ′′ ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑓 ∗ 𝑓 to 𝑐𝑥0 . We can construct such a path homotopy as follows. Draw a straight line from (𝑠, 𝑡) = (0, 0) to (𝑠, 𝑡) = (1∕2, 1) and a straight line from (𝑠, 𝑡) = (1, 0) to (𝑠, 𝑡) = (1∕2, 1). See Figure 7.8.

70 7.2. Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group

We may then define 𝐻 ′′ ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 by

⎧𝑥 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 𝑡∕2 ⎪ 0 ⎪𝑓 (2𝑠 − 𝑡) 𝑡∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 𝐻 ′′ (𝑠, 𝑡) = ⎨ ⎪𝑓 (2 − 2𝑠 − 𝑡) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 − 𝑡∕2 ⎪𝑥 1 − 𝑡∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. ⎩ 0

By Lemma 7.3, 𝐻 ′′ is continuous. Since { ′′ 𝑓 (2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 𝐻 (𝑠, 0) = 𝑓 (2 − 2𝑠) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1

it follows by Equation 7.4 that 𝐻 ′′ (𝑠, 0) = (𝑓 ∗ 𝑓 )(𝑠), and 𝐻 ′′ (𝑠, 1) = 𝑥0 = 𝑐𝑥0 (𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. Finally, since 𝐻 ′′ (0, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 and 𝐻 ′′ (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, it follows that 𝐻 ′′ is a path homotopy from 𝑓 ∗ 𝑓 to 𝑐𝑥0 . The construction of a path homotopy from 𝑓 ∗ 𝑓 to 𝑐𝑥1 is similar.

7.2 Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group The set of path homotopy classes of paths in a topological space 𝑋 does not form a group under the product of paths as the product of two path homotopy classes is not always defined. However, such a product is defined if we restrict to paths that begin and end at some fixed point 𝑥0 . This is the fundamental group of 𝑋 with basepoint 𝑥0 . We say that the ordered pair (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is a based space (sometimes also called a pointed space).

items.label: The fundamental group
items.formal_statement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space. A path 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 is called a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . Let 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) = {[𝑓 ] ∣ 𝑓 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 }
items.definition:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space. A path 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥0 is called a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . Let

𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) = {[𝑓 ] ∣ 𝑓 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 }

be the set of path homotopy classes of loops in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . We say that 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is the fundamental group of 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 .

From the definition it is clear that 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) depends only on the path component of 𝑋 that contains 𝑥0 . The fundamental group does not provide us with any information about the rest of 𝑋. As the notation suggests, the fundamental group is sometimes also referred to as the first homotopy group of 𝑋. In fact, there are groups 𝜋𝑛 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) for all 𝑛 ∈ ℤ+ . These groups are part of a subject called homotopy theory.

If we restrict Theorem 7.14 to the case where all paths are loops in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , we get the following result.
items.intuition: Defines the fundamental group and fixes terminology used later in fundamental-group, groups, homotopy.

metadata.source: chapter:7; section:7; sectionTitle:The fundamental group; number:7.15

items.label: The Fundamental Group Is a Group
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) be a based space. Then the fundamental group 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) of 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 is, in fact, a group with product of paths, ∗, as its binary operation. The identity element 𝑒 is equal to the path homotopy class of the constant path at 𝑥0 , 𝑒 = [𝑐𝑥0 ], and the inverse of [𝑓 ] is [𝑓 ]−1 = [𝑓 ] where 𝑓 is the reverse path of 𝑓 .

items.label: Fundamental Group of the Real Line
items.statement: Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ is a loop in ℝ based at 0, by Example 7.2 we know that 𝑓 is path homotopic to the constant path at 0. Thus 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) is the trivial group, i.e., 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) = {𝑒}. In general, 𝜋1 (ℝ𝑛 , 0) is the trivial group, where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology. We will often denote the trivial group as simply 0.

In general, 𝜋1 (ℝ𝑛 , 0) is the trivial group, where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology. We will often denote the trivial group as simply 0.

items.label: Fundamental Groups of Path-Connected Spaces Are Isomorphic
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is isomorphic to 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ).

Proof. Let 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based 𝑥0 , then 𝛼 ∗ 𝑓 ∗ 𝛼 ̂ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) by is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥1 . We define the map 𝛼

̂([𝑓 ]) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛼] 𝛼

where we have used the fact that [ 𝛼 ] = [𝛼]−1 . It is a well-defined map as ∗ is well-defined, and moreover, it only depends on the path homotopy class of 𝛼. We will show that 𝛼 ̂ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) is an isomorphism. We will first show that 𝛼 ̂ is a homomorphism, and then show that it is bijective. Since

̂([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔] ∗ [𝛼] 𝛼 = ([𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛼]) ∗ ([𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑔] ∗ [𝛼]) =𝛼 ̂([𝑓 ]) ∗ 𝛼 ̂([𝑔]),

for all [𝑓 ], [𝑔] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), it follows that 𝛼 ̂ is a homomorphism. To show that 𝛼 ̂ is bijective, and hence, an isomorphism, we show that 𝛼 ̂ has an inverse. Let 𝛽 = 𝛼, i.e., 𝛽 is the reverse path of 𝛼. Since ̂ 𝛽([ℎ]) = [𝛽]−1 ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛽] = [𝛼] ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛼]−1 𝛽([ℎ])) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ ([𝛽]−1 ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛽]) ∗ [𝛼] = [ℎ] ̂( ̂ 𝛼

for all [ℎ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ), and

̂([𝑓 ]) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛼] = [𝛽] ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛽]−1 𝛼 ̂ 𝛼 ([𝑓 ])) = [𝛽]−1 ∗ ([𝛼]−1 ∗ [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝛼]) ∗ [𝛽] = [𝑓 ] 𝛽(̂

𝛼 )−1 . for all [𝑓 ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), it follows that 𝛽̂ = (̂

In light of Theorem 7.19 it might be tempting to simply speak of the fundamental group of a path-connected space 𝑋, and thus leave out any mention of the basepoint. However, while 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) ≅ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) for any two points 𝑥0 and 𝑥1 in 𝑋, the isomorphism may depend on the path from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 . It can be shown that the isomorphism of 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) with 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) for a path-connected space 𝑋 is independent of path if and only if the fundamental group is abelian.

items.label: Simply connected spaces
items.formal_statement: A path-connected space $X$ is simply connected if $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is the trivial group for some, equivalently every, basepoint $x_0\in X$.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a path-connected space. We say that 𝑋 is simply connected if 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is the trivial group for some 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋, and hence, for all 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑋.

By Example 7.17, we get that ℝ𝑛 is simply connected where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard topology. We shall later see that the circle 𝑆 1 and the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 are two examples of non-simply connected spaces.
items.intuition: Simply connected spaces are path-connected spaces with no nontrivial loops up to path homotopy.

items.label: Based maps
items.formal_statement: Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. A based map ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is a continuous map ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 such that ℎ(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 . Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , the composition ℎ◦𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a loop in 𝑌 based at 𝑦0 . This leads to a map from 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) to 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).

items.label: Based Maps Induce Homomorphisms on Fundamental Groups
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be a based map. The map ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) given by ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ◦𝑓 ] is a homomorphism.

Proof. We need to show that for two loops 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , the identity

holds in 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ). The left-hand side is the path homotopy class of ℎ◦(𝑓 ∗ 𝑔) and the right-hand side is the path homotopy class of (ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔). By definition, we have { (ℎ◦𝑓 )(2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ((ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔))(𝑠) = (ℎ◦𝑔)(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1

and so { ℎ(𝑓 (2𝑠)) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (ℎ◦(𝑓 ∗ 𝑔))(𝑠) = ((ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔))(𝑠) = ℎ(𝑔(2𝑠 − 1)) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1. Hence, ℎ◦(𝑓 ∗ 𝑔) = (ℎ◦𝑓 ) ∗ (ℎ◦𝑔). Thus ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) ∗ ℎ∗ ([𝑔]) holds in 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).

items.label: Functoriality
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ), (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and (𝑍, 𝑧0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ1 ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and ℎ2 ∶ (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) → (𝑍, 𝑧0 ) be based maps. Then

(ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ = (ℎ2 )∗ ◦(ℎ1 )∗ .

If id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is the identity map, then (id𝑋 )∗ is the identity automorphism of 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ).

The rule sending a based space (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) to its fundamental group 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and a based map ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) to its induced homomorphism ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is a functor from the category of based spaces and based maps to the category of groups and (group) homomorphisms.

Proof. Assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 . Then

(ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [(ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )◦𝑓 ]

and ( ) (ℎ2 )∗ (ℎ1 )∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ2 ◦(ℎ1 ◦𝑓 )].

Since composition of continuous maps is associative, i.e., (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )◦𝑓 = ℎ2 ◦(ℎ1 ◦𝑓 ), these path homo- topy classes must be equal. Hence, (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ = (ℎ2 )∗ ◦(ℎ1 )∗ . If id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is the identity map, then id𝑋 ◦𝑓 = 𝑓 , and so, (id𝑋 )∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [id𝑋 ◦𝑓 ] = [𝑓 ]. Hence, (id𝑋 )∗ is the identity automorphism of 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ).

The following corollary of Theorem 7.24 tells us that the fundamental group is a topological in- variant of based spaces.

items.label: Based Homeomorphisms Induce Isomorphisms on Fundamental Groups
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. If ℎ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a homeomorphism such that ℎ(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 , then ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) is an isomorphism.

The fundamental group thus allows us to distinguish between two based spaces provided that their funda- mental groups are not isomorphic. By Example 7.17, we know that 𝜋1 (ℝ, 0) = 0 and 𝜋1 (ℝ2 , 0) = 0. However, by Example 6.16, we know that ℝ and ℝ2 are not homeomorphic. Hence, it is possible for two non-homeomorphic based spaces to have isomorphic fundamental groups.

Proof. We must show that ℎ∗ is bijective. Let 𝑘 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 be the inverse homemorphism of ℎ such that 𝑘(𝑦0 ) = 𝑥0 . Then, by Theorem 7.24, 𝑘∗ ◦ℎ∗ = (𝑘◦ℎ)∗ = (id𝑋 )∗ , and ℎ∗ ◦𝑘∗ = (ℎ◦𝑘)∗ = (id𝑌 )∗ . Hence, 𝑘∗ = (ℎ∗ )−1 .

To compute the fundamental group of a product space, we may use the following theorem.

The fundamental group thus allows us to distinguish between two based spaces provided that their fundamental groups are not isomorphic.

items.label: Fundamental Group of a Product Space
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces. Then 𝜋1 (𝑋×𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )) is isomorphic to the direct product 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ).

74 7.3. Homotopy type

Proof. Let pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑌 → 𝑋 and pr 2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝑌 → 𝑌 be the projection maps onto the first and second factor, respectively. Define the map 𝜑 ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋 × 𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) by ( ) ( ) 𝜑([ℎ]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) = [pr 1 ◦ℎ], [pr 2 ◦ℎ] .

Note that 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) × 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦𝑜 ) is a group with binary operation given by

([𝑓 ], [𝑔]) ∗ ([𝑓 ′ ], [𝑔 ′ ]) = ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑓 ′ ], [𝑔] ∗ [𝑔 ′ ])

for [𝑓 ], [𝑓 ′ ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and [𝑔], [𝑔 ′ ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ). We will show that 𝜑 is an isomorphism. It is a homomorphism since (pr 1 )∗ and (pr 2 )∗ are homo- morphisms: ( ) 𝜑([ℎ] ∗ [𝑘]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ] ∗ [𝑘]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ] ∗ [𝑘]) ( ) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 1 )∗ ([𝑘]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 2 )∗ ([𝑘]) ( ) ( ) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 1 )∗ ([𝑘]), (pr 2 )∗ ([𝑘]) = 𝜑([ℎ]) ∗ 𝜑([𝑘])

for [ℎ], [𝑘] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋 × 𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )). Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , and let 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a loop in 𝑌 based at 𝑦0 . Define the map ℎ ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 by

ℎ(𝑠) = (𝑓 (𝑠), 𝑔(𝑠)) .

Then ℎ is a loop in 𝑋 × 𝑌 based at (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 ), and ( ) ( ) 𝜑([ℎ]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) = [pr 1 ◦ℎ], [pr 2 ◦ℎ] = ([𝑓 ], [𝑔]).

Hence, 𝜑 is surjective. To show that 𝜑 is injective, assume that 𝜑([ℎ]) = 𝜑([𝑘]) for [ℎ], [𝑘] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋 × 𝑌 , (𝑥0 , 𝑦0 )). Then there is a path homotopy 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 from pr 1 ◦ℎ to pr 1 ◦𝑘 and a path homotopy 𝐻2 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 from pr 2 ◦ℎ to pr 2 ◦𝑘. Define the map 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 × 𝑌 by ( ) 𝐻(𝑠, 𝑡) = 𝐻1 (𝑠, 𝑡), 𝐻2 (𝑠, 𝑡) .

Then 𝐻 is a path homotopy from ℎ = (pr 1 ◦ℎ, pr 2 ◦ℎ) to 𝑘 = (pr 1 ◦𝑘, pr 2 ◦𝑘). Hence, [ℎ] = [𝑘], and so, 𝜑 is injective. Thus 𝜑 is an isomorphism.

7.3 Homotopy type We will see in this section how we may obtain information about the fundamental group by way of homotopy types. This allows us to compute the fundamental group of a topological space by computing the fundamental group of some other topological space that is hopefully easier to work with.

items.label: Based Homotopic Maps Induce the Same Homomorphism
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) and (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and 𝑘 ∶ (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) be based maps. If there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 from ℎ to 𝑘 such that 𝐻(𝑥0 , 𝑡) = 𝑦0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, then the homomorphisms ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) and 𝑘∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) induced by ℎ and 𝑘, respectively, are equal.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 75

Proof. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a loop in 𝑋 based at 𝑥0 , then 𝐻◦(𝑓 × id) ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a homotopy from ℎ◦𝑓 to 𝑘◦𝑓 where id ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐼 is the identity map of 𝐼. Since (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(𝑠, 0) = (ℎ◦𝑓 )(𝑠) and (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(𝑠, 1) = (𝑘◦𝑓 )(𝑠) for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(0, 𝑡) = 𝑦0 and (𝐻◦(𝑓 × id))(1, 𝑡) = 𝑦0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼, 𝐻◦(𝑓 × id) is a path homotopy from ℎ◦𝑓 to 𝑘◦𝑓 . Hence, ℎ∗ ([𝑓 ]) = [ℎ◦𝑓 ] = [𝑘◦𝑓 ] = 𝑘∗ ([𝑓 ]). Thus ℎ∗ = 𝑘∗ .

items.label: Retractions
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. We say that a continuous map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝑟(𝑎) = 𝑎 for each 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. If there is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴, we say that 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋.
items.definition: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. We say that a continuous map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝑟(𝑎) = 𝑎 for each 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴. If there is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴, we say that 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋.

items.label: The Circle Is a Retract of the Punctured Plane
items.statement:
The circle, 𝑆 1 , is a retract of ℝ2 ⧵ {0} where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Specifically, the continuous map 𝑟 ∶ ℝ2 ⧵ {0} → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑥 𝑟(𝑥) = ‖𝑥‖ √ where ‖𝑥‖ = 𝑥21 + 𝑥22 , is a retraction of ℝ2 ⧵ {0} onto 𝑆 1 .

If 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map, then we can express that 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 by the following commutative diagram. 𝑖 𝐴 𝑋 𝑟 id𝐴 𝐴

Hence, for a retraction 𝑟, we have 𝑟◦𝑖 = id𝐴 where id𝐴 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐴 is the identity map of 𝐴.

items.label: Retracts Induce Injective Fundamental Group Homomorphisms
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋, then the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is a monomorphism.

Proof. Let 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 be a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴. Then, by Theorem 7.24 and the definition of retraction, we have the following commutative diagram 𝑖∗ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝑟∗ (id𝐴 )∗ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 )

where id𝐴 is the identity map of 𝐴. By Theorem 7.24, we know that (id𝐴 )∗ is an isomorphism. Thus 𝑟∗ ◦𝑖∗ is an isomorphism, and hence, also bijective. This implies that 𝑖∗ is injective. Hence, 𝑖∗ is a monomorphism.

The homomorphism induced by the inclusion of 𝐴 into 𝑋 is an isomorphism if 𝐴 is a deformation retract.

items.label: Deformation retracts
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. A homotopy 𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 is called a deformation retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 𝑡) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We say that 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋. The map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 defined by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1) is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴. Thus 𝐻 is a homotopy between the identity map of 𝑋 and the map 𝑖◦𝑟 where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map. Note that some authors call what we have defined as a deformation retract, a strong deformation retract. In this terminology a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a deformation retract of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 1) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. A homotopy 𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑋 is called a deformation retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 𝑡) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We say that 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋.

The map 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 defined by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1) is a retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴. Thus 𝐻 is a homotopy between the identity map of 𝑋 and the map 𝑖◦𝑟 where 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is the inclusion map. Note that some authors call what we have defined as a deformation retract, a strong deformation retract. In this terminology a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a deformation retract of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 if 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑥 and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) ∈ 𝐴 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, and 𝐻(𝑎, 1) = 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴.

Every deformation retract is also a retract but the converse is not true in general.
items.intuition: Defines deformation retracts and fixes terminology used later in homotopy, subspace, topology.

metadata.source: chapter:7; section:7; sectionTitle:The fundamental group; number:7.31

items.label: Singleton Retracts and Path Connectedness Obstruction
items.statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝐴 = {𝑎} is retract of 𝑋 with retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 given by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝑎. If 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then 𝑋 must be path-connected: the deformation retraction gives that there is a path from each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 to 𝑎. However, there are path-connected spaces that do not deformation retract onto a point.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑎 ∈ 𝑋. Then 𝐴 = {𝑎} is retract of 𝑋 with retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 given by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝑎. If 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then 𝑋 must be path-connected: the deformation retraction gives that there is a path from each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 to 𝑎. However, there are path-connected spaces that do not deformation retract onto a point.

metadata.source: chapter:7; section:7; sectionTitle:The fundamental group; number:7.32

items.label: Deformation Retracts Induce Fundamental Group Isomorphisms
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a deformation retract of 𝑋, then the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋 is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 be a deformation retraction of 𝑋 onto 𝐴, and let a retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐴 of 𝑋 onto 𝐴 be given by 𝑟(𝑥) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1). By Lemma 7.27 and Theorem 7.24, we have 𝑖∗ ◦𝑟∗ = (id𝑋 )∗ where id𝑋 is the identity map of 𝑋. Since 𝑟◦𝑖 = id𝐴 , we have 𝑟∗ ◦𝑖∗ = (id𝐴 )∗ where id𝐴 is the identity map of 𝐴. Thus both 𝑟∗ ◦𝑖∗ and 𝑖∗ ◦𝑟∗ are isomorphisms. Hence, 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐴, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is an isomorphism with 𝑟∗ as its inverse.

We may generalize the notion of deformation retraction. This leads us to the notion of homotopy equivalence.

items.label: Homotopy equivalences
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 are continuous maps such that 𝑔◦𝑓 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑋, id𝑋 , and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑌 , id𝑌 , we say that 𝑓 and 𝑔 are homotopy equivalences. We say that each of 𝑓 and 𝑔 is a homotopy inverse of the other. Clearly, every homeomorphism is also a homotopy equivalence. The converse is not true in general.
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. If 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 are continuous maps such that 𝑔◦𝑓 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑋, id𝑋 , and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 is homotopic to the identity map of 𝑌 , id𝑌 , we say that 𝑓 and 𝑔 are homotopy equivalences. We say that each of 𝑓 and 𝑔 is a homotopy inverse of the other.

Clearly, every homeomorphism is also a homotopy equivalence. The converse is not true in general.
items.intuition: Defines homotopy equivalences and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homotopy, maps.

metadata.source: chapter:7; section:7; sectionTitle:The fundamental group; number:7.34

items.label: The Real Line Is Homotopy Equivalent to a Point
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then for any 𝑝 ∈ ℝ, the map 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → {𝑝} given by 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑝 is a homotopy equivalence with homotopy inverse 𝑔 ∶ {𝑝} → ℝ given by 𝑔(𝑝) = 𝑝. It is clear that 𝑓 is not a homeomorphism. In fact, there are no homeomorphisms between ℝ and {𝑝}.

items.label: Homotopy types
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. We say that 𝑋 and 𝑌 have the same homotopy type if there is a homotopy equivalence 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 . It is common to also refer to topological spaces of the same homotopy type as homotopy equivalent. We end this section with a result showing that topological spaces of the same homotopy type have isomorphic fundamental groups. To prove the result we will need
items.definition:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. We say that 𝑋 and 𝑌 have the same homotopy type if there is a homotopy equivalence 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 .

It is common to also refer to topological spaces of the same homotopy type as homotopy equivalent. We end this section with a result showing that topological spaces of the same homotopy type have isomorphic fundamental groups. To prove the result we will need the following lemma.

items.label: Homotopic Maps and Change of Basepoint
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be continuous maps such that 𝑓 (𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 and 𝑔(𝑥0 ) = 𝑦1 . If 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 is a homotopy from 𝑓 to 𝑔, there is a path 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 in 𝑌 from 𝑦0 to 𝑦1 given by 𝛼(𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥0 , 𝑡) such that 𝑔∗ = 𝛼 ̂◦𝑓∗ . 𝑓∗ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) 𝛼 ̂ 𝑔∗ 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦1 )

By Lemma 7.3, 𝐻 ′ is continuous. Since

and ⎧𝛼(4𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕4 ′ ⎪ 𝐻 (𝑠, 1) = ⎨𝐻(ℎ(4𝑠 − 1), 0) 1∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪𝛼(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎩ ⎧𝛼(4𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕4 ⎪ = ⎨𝑓 (ℎ(4𝑠 − 1)) 1∕4 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 ⎪𝛼(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1 ⎩

for all 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, and 𝐻 ′ (0, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥0 , 1) = 𝑦1 and 𝐻 ′ (1, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥0 , 1) = 𝑦1 , it follows that 𝐻 ′ is a path homotopy from 𝑔◦ℎ to 𝛼 ∗ (𝑓 ◦ℎ) ∗ 𝛼. Thus [𝑔◦ℎ] = [𝛼 ∗ (𝑓 ◦ℎ) ∗ 𝛼]. This completes the proof.

items.label: Homotopy Equivalences Induce Fundamental Group Isomorphisms
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a homotopy equivalence such that 𝑓 (𝑥0 ) = 𝑦0 . Then

𝑓∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 )

is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 be a homotopy inverse of 𝑓 with 𝑔(𝑦0 ) = 𝑥1 , and let 𝑓 (𝑥1 ) = 𝑦1 . By assump- tion, we have 𝑔◦𝑓 ≃ id𝑋 and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 ≃ id𝑌 where id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 and id𝑌 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑌 are the identity maps of 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively. Thus, by Lemma 7.37, there is a path 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 and a path 𝛽 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑌 from 𝑦0 to 𝑦1 such that

(𝑔◦(𝑓𝑥0 ))∗ = 𝑔∗ ◦(𝑓𝑥0 )∗ = 𝛼 ̂◦(id𝑋 )∗ = 𝛼 ̂

and

̂ 𝑌 )∗ = 𝛽̂ ((𝑓𝑥1 )◦𝑔)∗ = (𝑓𝑥1 )∗ ◦𝑔∗ = 𝛽◦(id

where we have specified the basepoint for the homomorphisms induced by 𝑓 . In other words, the following two diagrams commute. (id𝑋 )∗ 𝑔∗ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦0 ) 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) 𝛼 ̂ (𝑓𝑥1 )∗ 𝑔∗ ◦(𝑓𝑥0 )∗ 𝛽̂ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥1 ) 𝜋1 (𝑌 , 𝑦1 )

Since 𝛼̂ and 𝛽̂ are both isomorphisms, it follows by commutativity of the diagram on the left that 𝑔∗ is an epimorphism, and by commutativity of the diagram on the right that 𝑔∗ is a monomorphism. Hence, 𝑔∗ is an isomorphism. Since (𝑓𝑥0 )∗ = (𝑔∗ )−1 ◦̂ 𝛼 it follows that (𝑓𝑥0 )∗ is an isomorphism.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 79

We can use Theorem 7.38 to help us determine the fundamental group of certain topological spaces, and as a tool to determine that certain topological spaces are not of the same homotopy type, and hence, not homeo- morphic.

7.4 Exercises

Exercise 7.1 Let 𝑋, 𝑌 and 𝑍 be topological spaces. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is homotopic to 𝑓 ′ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍 is homotopic to 𝑔 ′ ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑍, then 𝑔◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑍 is homotopic to 𝑔 ′ ◦𝑓 ′ ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑍.

Exercise 7.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Show that 𝑋 is path-connected if and only if every two constant maps 𝑐1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 and 𝑐2 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 are homotopic.

Exercise 7.3 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 and 𝑥2 be points in 𝑋. Show that if 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path in 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥1 , 𝛽 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 is a path in 𝑋 from 𝑥1 to 𝑥2 and 𝛾 = 𝛼 ∗ 𝛽, ̂ 𝛼. then 𝛾̂ = 𝛽◦̂

Exercise 7.4 Let 𝑆 2 be the 2-sphere considered to be a subspace of ℝ3 with the standard topology. Show that 𝜋1 (𝑆 2 ⧵ {(0, 0, 1)}, 𝑠) is the trivial group where 𝑠 = (0, 0, −1).

Exercise 7.5 Let 𝑋 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Show that if 𝐴 is a retract of 𝑋, then 𝐴 is a closed subset of 𝑋.

Exercise 7.6 Show that the relation of homotopy equivalence is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.

A topological space 𝑋 is said to be contractible if the identity map of 𝑋, id𝑋 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋, is nullho- motopic.

Exercise 7.7 Show that a topological space 𝑋 is contractible if and only if 𝑋 has the homotopy type of a point, i.e., a topological space consisting of one point.

Exercise 7.8 Let 𝑋 be a topological space and let 𝑆 1 be the unit circle considered as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑆 1 is a continuous map that is not surjective, then 𝑓 is nullhomotopic.

8. The fundamental group of the circle

8.1 Covering spaces The notion of a covering space is a helpful tool for determining fundamental groups. Specifically, we will compute the fundamental group of the circle by first establishing that the map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 , given by 𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)),

is a covering map.

items.label: Covering spaces
items.formal_statement: Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. We say that a surjective continuous map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map if for each point 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 there is a neighborhood 𝑈 such that 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) is a disjoint union of open subsets 𝑉𝜆 of 𝐸 where 𝜆 ∈ Λ, ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , 𝜆∈Λ and 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. We refer to 𝐸 as a covering space of 𝐵.
items.definition:
Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. We say that a surjective continuous map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map if for each point 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 there is a neighborhood 𝑈 such that 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) is a disjoint union of open subsets 𝑉𝜆 of 𝐸 where 𝜆 ∈ Λ, ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , 𝜆∈Λ

and 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. We refer to 𝐸 as a covering space of 𝐵.

A covering map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is sometimes also referred to as a covering projection. Note that 𝐵 is often referred to as the base space of the covering map and that 𝐸 is often referred to as the total space of the covering map. An open subset 𝑈 of 𝐵 satisfying the condition that ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , where 𝑉𝜆 is an open subset of 𝐸, 𝜆∈Λ

such that 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ is said to be evenly covered by 𝑝. See Figure 8.1. For each 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵, we often refer to the preimage 𝑝−1 (𝑏) of {𝑏} under 𝑝 as the fiber over 𝑏. Note also that the fiber over 𝑏 is a discrete subspace of 𝐸; each 𝑉𝜆 is an open subset of 𝐸 and intersects the fiber over 𝑏 in a single point, and so, this point is open in the fiber over 𝑏. Finally, we note that a covering map is a special case of a fiber bundle with discrete fibers.
items.intuition: Defines covering spaces and fixes terminology used later in continuity, covering, maps.

items.label: Projection onto a Factor with Discrete Fiber Is a Covering Map
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐷 be a discrete space. Then the projection map onto the first factor, pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐷 → 𝑋, where 𝑋 × 𝐷 is given the product topology, is a covering map. In particular, 𝑋 is evenly covered by pr 1 .

items.label: Local homeomorphisms
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is said to be a local homeomorphism if for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a neighborhood 𝑈 such that 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 |𝑈 ∶ 𝑈 → 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is a homeomorphism where 𝑈 and 𝑓 (𝑈 ) are both given the subspace topology from 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively.

A continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is said to be a local homeomorphism if for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a neighborhood 𝑈 such that 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 |𝑈 ∶ 𝑈 → 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is a homeomorphism where 𝑈 and 𝑓 (𝑈 ) are both given the subspace topology from 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively.

items.label: Homeomorphisms Are Local Homeomorphisms
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a homeomorphism. Then 𝑓 is also a local homeomorphism.

Local homeomorphisms preserve local topological properties such as local connectedness and local path- connectedness. In other words, if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a local homeomorphism and, say, 𝑋 is locally connected then so is 𝑌 . A topological space 𝑋 is locally (path) connected at 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 if for each neighborhood 𝑈 of 𝑥 there is a (path) connected neighborhood 𝑉 of 𝑥 such that 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑈 . We say that 𝑋 is locally (path) connected if 𝑋 is locally (path) connected at each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Note that a (path) connected space need not be locally (path) connected. Clearly, a covering map is also a local homeomorphism. Thus if 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map, then the total space and the base space are locally equivalent but they can differ globally.

The following theorem provides an important example of a covering space that we will use later on to compute the fundamental group of the circle.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a homeomorphism. Then 𝑓 is also a local homeomorphism.
items.intuition: Illustrates homeomorphisms are local homeomorphisms in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: The Exponential Map Covers the Circle
items.statement:
Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider the circle 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Then the map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)) is a covering map.

82 8.1. Covering spaces

Proof. Clearly, 𝑝 is continuous and surjective. Let 𝑈 = 𝑆 1 ⧵ {(−1, 0)} and 𝑉 = 𝑆 1 ⧵ {(1, 0)}. Then both 𝑈 and 𝑉 are open in 𝑆 1 , and their union is equal to 𝑆 1 . We want to show that they are evenly covered by 𝑝. The preimage of 𝑈 under 𝑝 is the disjoint union of subsets 𝑉𝜆 = (𝜆 − 1∕2, 𝜆 + 1∕2) of ℝ for 𝜆 ∈ ℤ; ⨆ ⨆( 1 1 ) 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 = 𝜆 − ,𝜆 + . 𝜆∈ℤ 𝜆∈ℤ 2 2

Similarly, the preimage of 𝑉 under 𝑝 is the disjoint union of subsets 𝑊𝜆 = (𝜆, 𝜆 + 1) of ℝ for 𝜆 ∈ ℤ; ⨆ ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑊𝜆 = (𝜆, 𝜆 + 1). 𝜆∈ℤ 𝜆∈ℤ

Since 𝑝 is continuous and open, cf. [5, Lemma 14.33], and 𝑉𝜆 and 𝑊𝜆 are open subsets of ℝ, it follows that 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 and 𝑝|𝑊𝜆 ∶ 𝑊𝜆 → 𝑉 are open maps for each 𝜆 ∈ ℤ. Moreover, they are both also bijective for each 𝜆 ∈ ℤ. Hence, by Theorem 5.21 they are both homeomorphisms. Thus 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 is a covering map.

The covering map 𝑝 from Theorem 8.6 is illustrated in Figure 8.2. Theorem 8.6 also provides an example of a universal covering space since ℝ is simply connected. It is universal in the following sense: for any covering map 𝑞 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝑆 1 where we assume that 𝐸 is path-connected, there is a covering map 𝑟 ∶ ℝ → 𝐸 such that 𝑝 = 𝑞◦𝑟. 𝑟 ℝ 𝐸

items.label: Products of Covering Maps Are Covering Maps
items.statement:
Let 𝐵1 , 𝐵2 , 𝐸1 and 𝐸2 be topological spaces, and let 𝑝1 ∶ 𝐸1 → 𝐵1 and 𝑝2 ∶ 𝐸2 → 𝐵2 be covering maps. Then

𝑝1 × 𝑝2 ∶ 𝐸1 × 𝐸2 → 𝐵1 × 𝐵2

is a covering map where both 𝐵1 × 𝐵2 and 𝐸1 × 𝐸2 are given the product topology.

Proof. Let (𝑏1 , 𝑏2 ) ∈ 𝐵1 × 𝐵2 and let 𝑈1 and 𝑈2 be neighborhoods of 𝑏1 and 𝑏2 , respectively, that are covered evenly by 𝑝1 and 𝑝2 , respectively. We want to show that 𝑈1 × 𝑈2 is evenly covered by 𝑝1 × 𝑝2 . If ⨆ ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 1 ) = 𝑉 𝜆 and 𝑝 −1 (𝑈 2 ) = 𝑊𝜔 𝜆∈Λ 𝜔∈Ω

then, clearly, ⨆( ( )−1 ) 𝑝1 × 𝑝2 (𝑈1 × 𝑈2 ) = 𝑝−1 (𝑈 1 ) × 𝑝 −1 (𝑈 2 ) = 𝑉𝜆 × 𝑊𝜔 . 𝜆∈Λ 𝜔∈Ω

Furthermore, 𝑝1 × 𝑝2 |𝑉𝜆 ×𝑊𝜔 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 × 𝑊𝜔 → 𝑈1 × 𝑈2 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ and each 𝜔 ∈ Ω. Hence, 𝑈1 × 𝑈2 is a neighborhood of (𝑏1 , 𝑏2 ) that is evenly covered by 𝑝1 × 𝑝2 . Thus 𝑝1 × 𝑝2 is a covering map.

items.label: The Plane Covers the Torus
items.statement:
From Theorem 8.6 and Theorem 8.7, it follows that

𝑝 × 𝑝 ∶ ℝ × ℝ → 𝑆1 × 𝑆1

where 𝑝 is the covering map of Theorem 8.6, is a covering map. Hence, ℝ2 is a (universal) covering space of the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 .

8.2 Computing the fundamental group of the circle

In this section we will see how we can use covering spaces as a tool to compute fundamental groups. In particular, we will compute the fundamental group of the circle. By Theorem 8.6, we know that the map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)) is a covering map. In order to compute 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) we will show that the lifting correspondence

Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) → 𝑝−1 (𝑠0 ) = ℤ,

where 𝑠0 = (1, 0), is a bijection, and moreover, that it is an isomorphism of groups. This will involve certain lifting theorems. Let 𝐵, 𝐸 and 𝑋 be topological spaces, and let 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 be a continuous map. If we are given a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝐵, we are often interested in whether or not there is a continuous map 𝑓̃∶ 𝑋 → 𝐸 such that 𝑓 = 𝑝◦𝑓̃.

We will focus on the case where 𝑝 is assumed to be a covering map. We will need a theorem that says that any path in the base space of a covering map can be lifted to a path in the total space. items.label: Lebesgue number lemma
items.statement:
Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a compact metric space, and let  be an open cover of 𝑋. Then there is a real number 𝜆 > 0 such that for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a 𝑈 ∈  where 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) ⊆ 𝑈 .

The number 𝜆 is called a Lebesgue number for the open cover . Note that a Lebesgue number 𝜆 for an open cover  is not unique as any positive real number less than 𝜆 is also a Lebesgue number for .

Proof. If 𝑋 ∈ , then every real number 𝜆 > 0 will be a Lebesgue number for . So assume that 𝑋 is not an element of . By compactness, there is a finite subcollection, say, {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } of  that covers 𝑋. For each 𝑖 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛}, let 𝑉𝑖 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈𝑖 . Then each 𝑉𝑖 is non-empty as 𝑈𝑖 ≠ 𝑋 for all 𝑖 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛}. Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology (and the standard Euclidean metric), and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be given by 𝑛 1∑ 𝑓 (𝑥) = dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥) 𝑛 𝑖=1

where dist 𝑉𝑖 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ is given by

dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥) = inf {𝑑(𝑥, 𝑣) ∣ 𝑣 ∈ 𝑉𝑖 }.

We claim that dist 𝑉𝑖 is continuous for each 𝑖 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛}. Let 𝑥1 and 𝑥2 be two points in 𝑋. Then by M3, we have dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥1 ) ⩽ 𝑑(𝑥1 , 𝑣) ⩽ 𝑑(𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ) + 𝑑(𝑥2 , 𝑣), for all 𝑣 ∈ 𝑉𝑖 , and so, dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥1 ) − 𝑑(𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ) ⩽ dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥2 ). Similarly, we have dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥2 ) − 𝑑(𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ) ⩽ dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥1 ). Hence, | dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥1 ) − dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥2 )| ⩽ 𝑑(𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ). Thus dist 𝑉𝑖 is continuous for each 𝑖 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛}. Hence, 𝑓 is also continuous.

Chapter 8. The fundamental group of the circle 85

We claim that 𝑓 (𝑥) > 0 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Choose an 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Since {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } covers 𝑋, there must be a 𝑗 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛} such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑗 . Since 𝑈𝑗 ∈ , we know that 𝑈𝑗 must be an open set. Thus there is an 𝜖 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜖) ⊆ 𝑈𝑗 , and hence, dist 𝑉𝑗 (𝑥) > 𝜖. It follows that 𝑓 (𝑥) > 𝜖∕𝑛 > 0. Thus 𝑓 (𝑥) > 0 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 as claimed. Since 𝑓 is continuous with a compact domain and 𝑓 (𝑥) > 0 for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, it follows by Theo- rem 6.43 that 𝑓 must have a minimum value 𝜆 where 𝜆 > 0. We need to show that 𝜆 is a Lebesgue number for {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 }, and hence, for . Choose an 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Suppose that there is no 𝑈𝑖 ∈ {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 . Then dist 𝑉𝑖 (𝑥) < 𝜆 for all 𝑖 ∈ {1, 2, … , 𝑛}, and thus 𝑓 (𝑥) < 𝜆. This contradicts the fact that 𝜆 is the minimum value of 𝑓 over 𝑋. Hence, there must be a 𝑈𝑖 ∈  such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 . Thus 𝜆 is a Lebesgue number as claimed.

The number 𝜆 is called a Lebesgue number for the open cover . Note that a Lebesgue number 𝜆 for an open cover  is not unique as any positive real number less than 𝜆 is also a Lebesgue number for .

items.label: Lebesgue Number for a Cover of the Unit Interval
items.statement: Let 𝐼 = [0, 1] ⊆ ℝ where ℝ is given the standard Euclidean metric. Then 𝜆 = 1∕4 is a Lebesgue number for the open cover  = {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 } of 𝐼 where 𝑈1 = (−3, 5∕6) and 𝑈2 = (0, 2): if 0 ⩽ 𝑥 ⩽ 1∕2 then 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) = (𝑥 − 𝜆, 𝑥 + 𝜆) = (𝑥 − 1∕4, 𝑥 + 1∕4) ⊆ 𝑈1 , and if 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑥 ⩽ 1 then 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) ⊆ 𝑈2 .
items.formal_statement: Let 𝐼 = [0, 1] ⊆ ℝ where ℝ is given the standard Euclidean metric. Then 𝜆 = 1∕4 is a Lebesgue number for the open cover  = {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 } of 𝐼 where 𝑈1 = (−3, 5∕6) and 𝑈2 = (0, 2): if 0 ⩽ 𝑥 ⩽ 1∕2 then 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) = (𝑥 − 𝜆, 𝑥 + 𝜆) = (𝑥 − 1∕4, 𝑥 + 1∕4) ⊆ 𝑈1 , and if 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑥 ⩽ 1 then 𝖡(𝑥; 𝜆) ⊆ 𝑈2 .
items.intuition: Illustrates lebesgue number for a cover of the unit interval in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

items.label: Unique path lifting property
items.statement:
Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces, and let 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 be a covering map such that 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑏0 where 𝑒0 ∈ 𝐸. For any path 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 where 𝑓 (0) = 𝑏0 there is a unique path 𝑓̃∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 lifting 𝑓 such that 𝑓̃(0) = 𝑒0 .

Proof. For each 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼, let 𝑈𝑠 be a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑠) that is evenly covered by 𝑝. The collection  = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝑠 ) ∣ 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼} is then an open cover of 𝐼. Since 𝐼 is a compact metric space, it follows from Lemma 8.10 that there is a Lebesgue number 𝜆 > 0 for . Consider a subdivision of 𝐼 consisting of points 0 = 𝑠0 < 𝑠1 < ⋯ < 𝑠𝑛 = 1 such that 𝑠𝑖 − 𝑠𝑖−1 < 2𝜆 for 1 ⩽ 𝑖 ⩽ 𝑛. Then each interval [𝑠𝑖−1 , 𝑠𝑖 ] lies in one of the preimages 𝑓 −1 (𝑈𝑠 ). Thus 𝑓 ([𝑠𝑖−1 , 𝑠𝑖 ]) ⊆ 𝑈𝑠 for some 𝑠 ∈ 𝐼. We must show that there is a lift 𝑓̃ of 𝑓 such that 𝑓̃(0) = 𝑒0 and that this lift is unique. We prove the existence of 𝑓̃ first. Assume that [𝑎, 𝑏] ⊆ 𝐼. Let 𝑈 be a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑎) that is evenly covered by 𝑝 such that 𝑓 ([𝑎, 𝑏]) ⊆ 𝑈 , and let ⨆ 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜔 . 𝜔∈Ω

Note that if 𝑒 ∈ 𝑝−1 (𝑓 (𝑎)) then 𝑒 ∈ 𝑉𝜔′ for a unique 𝜔′ ∈ Ω. Since 𝑝|𝑉𝜔′ ∶ 𝑉𝜔′ → 𝑈 is a homeomor- phism, it follows that the map ( )−1 𝑔̃ = 𝑝|𝑉𝜔′ ◦𝑓 |[𝑎,𝑏] ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝐸

is continuous, and moreover, 𝑝◦̃𝑔 = 𝑓 |[𝑎,𝑏] and 𝑔̃(𝑎) = 𝑒. From this observation we get that there is a continuous map ( )−1 𝑔̃1 = 𝑝|𝑉𝜔 ◦𝑓 |[𝑠0 ,𝑠1 ] ∶ [𝑠0 , 𝑠1 ] → 𝐸

where 𝑝◦̃𝑔1 = 𝑓 |[𝑠0 ,𝑠1 ] and 𝑔̃1 (𝑠0 ) = 𝑒0 . Now let 𝑒1 = 𝑔̃1 (𝑠1 ) and proceed as before. That is, 𝑔̃2 = ( )−1 𝑝|𝑉𝜔 ◦𝑓 |[𝑠1 ,𝑠2 ] ∶ [𝑠1 , 𝑠2 ] → 𝐸 is a continuous map where 𝑝◦̃ 𝑔2 = 𝑓 |[𝑠1 ,𝑠2 ] and 𝑔̃2 (𝑠1 ) = 𝑒1 .

86 8.2. Computing the fundamental group of the circle

Continuing this process, we get a continuous map ( )−1 𝑔̃𝑖 = 𝑝|𝑉𝜔 ◦𝑓 |[𝑠𝑖−1 ,𝑠𝑖 ] ∶ [𝑠𝑖−1 , 𝑠𝑖 ] → 𝐸 𝑖−1

𝑔𝑖 = 𝑓 |[𝑠𝑖−1 ,𝑠𝑖 ] and and where 𝑝◦̃ 𝑔̃𝑖 (𝑠𝑖−1 ) = 𝑔̃𝑖−1 (𝑠𝑖−1 ) = 𝑒𝑖−1 for 𝑖 ∈ {3, 4, … , 𝑛}. By Lemma 7.3, we can combine 𝑔̃1 , 𝑔̃2 , … , 𝑔̃𝑛 into a continuous map

𝑓̃∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸

given by 𝑓̃(𝑠) = 𝑔̃𝑖 (𝑠) for 𝑠 ∈ [𝑠𝑖−1 , 𝑠𝑖 ]. Then 𝑓̃ is a lift of 𝑓 such that 𝑓̃(0) = 𝑒0 . We conclude the proof by showing the uniqueness of 𝑓̃. Assume that there are two continuous maps 𝑓̃1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 and 𝑓̃2 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 such that 𝑝◦𝑓̃1 = 𝑝◦𝑓̃2 = 𝑓 and 𝑓̃1 (0) = 𝑓̃2 (0) = 𝑒0 . We want to show that 𝑓̃1 = 𝑓̃2 . Let

𝐴 = {𝑠 ∈ 𝐼 ∣ 𝑓̃1 (𝑠) = 𝑓̃2 (𝑠)}

and

𝐷 = {𝑠 ∈ 𝐼 ∣ 𝑓̃1 (𝑠) ≠ 𝑓̃2 (𝑠)}.

Then clearly, 𝐼 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐷 and 𝐴 ∩ 𝐷 = ∅. If we can show that both 𝐴 are 𝐷 are open, then because 𝐼 is connected we have 𝐴 = 𝐼 and 𝐷 = ∅ as 0 ∈ 𝐴, and hence, 𝑓̃1 = 𝑓̃2 . Let 𝑠 ∈ 𝐴, and let 𝑈 be a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑠) that is evenly covered by 𝑝. Assume that 𝑉𝜔 is the subset of 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) that contains 𝑓̃1 (𝑠) = 𝑓̃2 (𝑠). Let  = 𝑓̃1−1 (𝑉𝜔 ) ∩ 𝑓̃2−1 (𝑉𝜔 ). Then  is a ̃ ̃ neighborhood ( ) ( both) 𝑓1 and 𝑓2 map all points in  to 𝑉𝜔 . Thus for all 𝑡 ∈  , we have of 𝑠 and 𝑝◦𝑓̃1 (𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑡) = 𝑝◦𝑓̃2 (𝑡) implying that 𝑓̃1 (𝑡) = 𝑓̃2 (𝑡) as 𝑝|𝑉 ∶ 𝑉𝜔 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism. 𝜔 Hence, 𝑡 ∈ 𝐴 and  ⊆ 𝐴. Thus 𝐴 is open. Finally, we need to show that 𝐷 is open. Choose an 𝑠 ∈ 𝐷. Let 𝑈 be a neighborhood of 𝑓 (𝑠) that is evenly covered by 𝑝. Since 𝑓̃1 (𝑠) ≠ 𝑓̃2 (𝑠), there are unique indexes 𝜔1 and 𝜔2 in Ω such that 𝑓̃1 (𝑠) ∈ 𝑉𝜔1 and 𝑓̃2 (𝑠) ∈ 𝑉𝜔2 . Then  ′ = 𝑓̃1−1 (𝑉𝜔1 ) ∩ 𝑓̃2−1 (𝑉𝜔2 ) is a neighborhood of 𝑠. Since 𝑉𝜔1 and 𝑉𝜔2 are disjoint, it follows that 𝑓̃1 (𝑡) ≠ 𝑓̃2 (𝑡) for all 𝑡 ∈  ′ . Thus  ′ ⊆ 𝐷. Hence, 𝐷 is open. Thus 𝑓̃1 = 𝑓̃2 .

items.label: Homotopy lifting property
items.statement:
Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces, and let 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 be a covering map such that 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑏0 where 𝑒0 ∈ 𝐸. If 𝐻 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝐵 is ̃ ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝐸 with a continuous map where 𝐻(0, 0) = 𝑏0 , then there is a unique lifting 𝐻 ̃ 𝐻(0, 0) = 𝑒0 .

𝐸 ̃ 𝐻 𝑝

𝐼 ×𝐼 𝐵 𝐻

̃ is a path homotopy. Furthermore, if 𝐻 is a path homotopy then 𝐻

Chapter 8. The fundamental group of the circle 87

Proof. We prove the statement about path homotopies. The proof of the first part of the theorem is similar to the proof of Theorem 8.12, where we make use of Lemma 8.10 to break 𝐼 × 𝐼 into smaller squares [𝑠𝑖−1 , 𝑠𝑖 ] × [𝑡𝑖−1 , 𝑡𝑖 ] that are mapped by 𝐻 into open sets of 𝐵 that are evenly covered by 𝑝. See [4, pp. 343–344] for details. Assume that 𝐻 is a path homotopy from the path 𝑓0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 to the path 𝑓1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 where 𝑓0 (0) = 𝑓1 (0) = 𝑏0 and 𝑓0 (1) = 𝑓1 (1) = 𝑏1 . Then the map 𝐻0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 given by

𝐻0 (𝑡) = 𝐻(0, 𝑡) ̃ 0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 given by is the constant path in 𝐵 at 𝑏0 . Hence, the map 𝐻 ̃ 0 (𝑡) = 𝐻(0, 𝐻 ̃ 𝑡)

is a lift of 𝐻0 where 𝐻 ̃ 0 (0) = ̃ 𝐻(0, 0) = 𝑒0 . Note that the constant path 𝑐𝑒0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 at 𝑒0 is also a lift of 𝐻0 such that 𝑐𝑒0 (0) = 𝑒0 . Thus by uniqueness 𝐻 ̃ 0 = 𝑐𝑒 . In other words, ̃ 𝐻(0, 𝑡) = 𝑒0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Similarly, let 𝐻1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 be the map given by

𝐻1 (𝑡) = 𝐻(1, 𝑡). ̃ 0) = 𝑒1 . Then the map 𝐻 Then 𝐻1 is the constant path 𝑐𝑏1 in 𝐵 at 𝑏1 . Let 𝐻(1, ̃ 1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 given by

̃ 1 (𝑡) = ̃ 𝐻 𝐻(1, 𝑡)

is a lift of 𝐻1 where 𝐻 ̃ 1 (0) = 𝐻(1, ̃ 0) = 𝑒1 . As the constant path 𝑐𝑒 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 at 𝑒1 is also a lift of ̃ 1 = 𝑐𝑒 . In other words, 𝐻(1, 𝐻1 such that 𝑐𝑒1 (0) = 𝑒1 , it follows by uniqueness that 𝐻 ̃ 𝑡) = 𝑒1 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Thus 𝐻 ̃ is a path homotopy from 𝑓̃0 to 𝑓̃1 .

In order to compute the fundamental group of the circle we will use a map referred to as the lifting correspondence that uses Theorem 8.13.

items.label: Lifting correspondences
items.formal_statement: Let $p:E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. For a loop $f:I\to B$ based at $b_0$, let $\widetilde f$ be the unique lift with $\widetilde f(0)=e_0$. The lifting correspondence sends $[f]\in\pi_1(B,b_0)$ to $\widetilde f(1)\in p^{-1}(b_0)$.
items.definition:
Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces, and let 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 be a covering map such that 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑏0 where 𝑒0 ∈ 𝐸. Furthermore, let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 be a loop in 𝐵 at 𝑏0 , and let 𝑓̃∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 be the unique lift of 𝑓 to a path in 𝐸 such that 𝑓̃(0) = 𝑒0 . We say that the map Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐵, 𝑏0 ) → 𝑝−1 (𝑏0 ) given by Φ([𝑓 ]) = 𝑓̃(1), is the lifting correspondence derived from 𝑝.

Let 𝑓0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 and 𝑓1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 be two loops in 𝐵 at 𝑏0 that are path homotopic. Then, by Theorem 8.13, the corresponding lifted paths 𝑓̃0 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 and 𝑓̃1 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 in 𝐸, where 𝑓̃0 (0) = 𝑓̃1 (0) = 𝑒0 , are also path homotopic. In particular, 𝑓̃0 (1) = 𝑓̃1 (1). Thus Φ is a well-defined map. We also note that Φ depends on the choice of 𝑒0 but we usually suppress this dependence in the notation, and hence, write Φ instead of Φ𝑒0 .
items.intuition: The lifting correspondence records where lifted loops end in the fiber over the basepoint, connecting covering spaces to fundamental groups.

metadata.source: chapter:8; section:8; sectionTitle:The fundamental group of the circle; number:8.14

items.label: Surjectivity of the Lifting Correspondence
items.statement:
Let 𝐵 be a topological space and let 𝐸 be a path-connected space. If 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map such that 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑏0 where 𝑒0 ∈ 𝐸, then the lifting correspondence

Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐵, 𝑏0 ) → 𝑝−1 (𝑏0 )

is surjective. If we assume that 𝐸 is simply connected, then Φ is a bijection.

88 8.2. Computing the fundamental group of the circle

Proof. Assume that 𝐸 is path-connected. We want to show that Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐵, 𝑏0 ) → 𝑝−1 (𝑏0 ) is surjective. In other words, we want to show that for every point 𝑒 ∈ 𝑝−1 (𝑏0 ) there is a loop 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 in 𝐵 based at 𝑏0 such that Φ([𝑓 ]) = 𝑒. Since 𝐸 is path-connected there is a path 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐸 in 𝐸 from 𝑒0 to 𝑒. Let 𝑓 = 𝑝◦𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵. Then 𝑓 (0) = 𝑝(𝑔(0)) = 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑏0 and 𝑓 (1) = 𝑝(𝑔(1)) = 𝑝(𝑒) = 𝑏0 . Hence, 𝑓 is a loop in 𝐵 based at 𝑏0 and, by Theorem 8.12, 𝑔 is the unique lift of 𝑓 to a path in 𝐸 starting at 𝑒0 , i.e., 𝑔 = 𝑓̃. Thus Φ([𝑓 ]) = 𝑓̃(1) = 𝑒. Hence, Φ is surjective. Now assume that 𝐸 is simply connected. We want to show that in this case Φ is also injective. In other words, if 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐵 are two loops in 𝐵 based at 𝑏0 where Φ([𝑓 ]) = Φ([𝑔]) = 𝑒, then we want to show that [𝑓 ] = [𝑔]. Let 𝑓̃ and 𝑔̃ be the unique lifts of 𝑓 and 𝑔, respectively, such that 𝑓̃(0) = 𝑔̃(0) = 𝑒0 . Since we have assumed that Φ([𝑓 ]) = Φ([𝑔]) = 𝑒, it follows that 𝑓̃(1) = 𝑔̃(1) = 𝑒. By taking the product of 𝑓̃ and the reverse of 𝑔̃, 𝑓̃ ∗ 𝑔̃, we get a loop in 𝐸 based at 𝑒0 . Since 𝐸 is simply connected there must be a path homotopy 𝐻 from 𝑓̃ ∗ 𝑔̃ to the constant path 𝑐𝑒0 in 𝐸 at 𝑒0 . It follows that the composition 𝑝◦𝐻 is a path homotopy from 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 to the constant path 𝑐𝑏0 in 𝐵 at 𝑏0 . Thus [𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]−1 = 𝑒 where 𝑒 denotes the identity element in the group 𝜋1 (𝐵, 𝑏0 ), and hence, [𝑓 ] = [𝑔]. Hence, Φ is injective and thus bijective.

We now have all the tools we need to compute the fundamental group of the circle.

items.label: The Fundamental Group of the Circle Is Isomorphic to the Integers
items.statement:
Let 𝑆 1 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥2 +𝑦2 = 1} be the circle considered as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Then for any 𝑠0 ∈ 𝑆 1 , the lifiting correspondence

Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) → ℤ

is an isomorphism where ℤ is the additive group of integers.

Proof. Since 𝑆 1 is path-connected, it follows by Theorem 7.19 that we only need to compute 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) for one basepoint (and hence, all) 𝑠0 ∈ 𝑆 1 . By Theorem 8.6, we know that the map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)) is a covering map. Furthermore, since ℝ is simply connected, it follows by Theorem 8.15 that the lifting correspondence

Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) → 𝑝−1 (𝑠0 )

is a bijection. Let 𝑒0 = 0 such that 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑝(0) = (1, 0) = 𝑠0 . Clearly, 𝑝−1 (𝑠0 ) = ℤ. We want to show that Φ is a homomorphism. In other words, if [𝑓 ], [𝑔] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) we want to show that Φ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = Φ([𝑓 ]) + Φ([𝑔]). Assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 and 𝑔 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 are two loops in 𝑆 1 based at 𝑠0 . By Theorem 8.12 there are unique lifts 𝑓̃ and 𝑔̃ of 𝑓 and 𝑔, respectively, such that 𝑓̃(0) = 𝑔̃(0) = 0. Let 𝑓̃(1) = 𝑥 and 𝑔̃(1) = 𝑦 such that, by definition of Φ, we have Φ([𝑓 ]) = 𝑥 and Φ([𝑔]) = 𝑦. We will show that Φ([𝑓 ∗ 𝑔]) = 𝑥 + 𝑦. To compute Φ([𝑓 ∗ 𝑔]) we must lift 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 to a path 𝑓̃ ∗ 𝑔 in ℝ such that (𝑓̃ ∗ 𝑔)(0) = 0. Let ̃ ℎ ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ be a path in ℝ given by

̃ ℎ(𝑠) = 𝑓̃(1) + 𝑔̃(𝑠) = 𝑥 + 𝑔̃(𝑠).

Note that ̃ ℎ is a lift of 𝑔 starting at 𝑥. Let 𝑓̃ ∗ ̃ ℎ ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ be a path in ℝ given by { 𝑓̃(2𝑠) 0 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1∕2 (𝑓̃ ∗ ̃ ℎ)(𝑠) = ̃ ℎ(2𝑠 − 1) 1∕2 ⩽ 𝑠 ⩽ 1.

Chapter 8. The fundamental group of the circle 89

Since 𝑝(𝑓̃ ∗ ̃ ℎ) = 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔, it follows that 𝑓̃ ∗ ̃ ℎ is the unique lift of 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 such that (𝑓̃ ∗ ̃ ℎ)(0) = 0. In other words, 𝑓 ∗ 𝑔 = 𝑓 ∗ ℎ. Finally, since Φ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = Φ([𝑓 ∗ 𝑔]) = (𝑓̃ ̃ ̃ ̃ ∗ 𝑔)(1) = ̃ℎ(1) = 𝑥 + 𝑔̃(1) = 𝑥 + 𝑦, it follows that Φ([𝑓 ] ∗ [𝑔]) = Φ([𝑓 ]) + Φ([𝑔]). Thus Φ is a homomorphism.

As an application of Theorem 8.16, we can prove the Brouwer fixed point theorem in dimension 2, cf. Theorem 1.1.

items.label: Brouwer fixed point theorem in dimension 2
items.statement:
Let 𝐷2 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 ⩽ 1} be the disk considered as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Then every continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝐷2 → 𝐷2 has a fixed point, i.e., there is an 𝑥 ∈ 𝐷2 such that 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑥.

Proof. Assume that 𝑓 has no fixed points. Then there is a retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝐷2 → 𝑆 1 given by having 𝑟(𝑥) be the point on the ray that originates at 𝑓 (𝑥) and passes through 𝑥 that lies on the circle. See Figure 8.3.

𝑆1

𝑓 (𝑥)

𝑥

𝑟(𝑥)

Figure 8.3: The map 𝑟 ∶ 𝐷2 → 𝑆 1 .

By Lemma 7.30, the homomorphism 𝑖∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 𝑠0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝐷2 , 𝑠0 ) induced by the inclusion map 𝑖 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝐷2 must be a monomorphism. Since 𝐷2 is simply connected (it has the homotopy type of a point), it follows by Theorem 8.16 that the homomorphism 𝑖∗ cannot be a monomorphism. Hence, there is no retraction 𝑟 ∶ 𝐷2 → 𝑆 1 .

8.3 The fundamental theorem of algebra The fundamental theorem of algebra, cf. Theorem 1.2, can be proved in many different ways. In fact, there is a book, [2], that is entirely dedicated into proving the fundamental theorem of algebra and that provides a total of twelve different ways (including a modern version of Gauss’s original proof from 1799) of proving the theorem! These proofs use techniques from abstract algebra, complex analysis and topology. We will now present one proof (which is not identical with any of the ones given in [2]) that uses what we know about the fundamental group of the circle. The proof we give here borrows freely from the one found in [4, §56] and it will consist of four steps. Throughout this section we will think of 𝑆 1 as the unit circle in the complex numbers ℂ. In other words, 𝑆 1 is the set of points in ℂ with modulus

90 8.3. The fundamental theorem of algebra

1, i.e., 𝑆 1 = {𝑧 ∈ ℂ ∣ |𝑧| = 1}. We consider 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℂ where ℂ ≅ ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Step 1: Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝑓 (𝑧) = 𝑧𝑛 where 𝑛 ∈ ℤ+ . Then the induced homo- morphism 𝑓∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) is a monomorphism (note that 1 ∈ ℂ corresponds to 𝑠0 = (1, 0) ∈ ℝ2 ). Step 2: Let 𝑔 ∶ 𝑆 1 → ℂ ⧵ {0} be the map given by 𝑔(𝑧) = 𝑧𝑛 . Then 𝑔 is not nullhomotopic, i.e., 𝑔 is not homotopic to a constant map. Step 3: Special case. Let 𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0 be a polynomial equation with coefficients in ℂ where we assume that |𝑎𝑛−1 | + |𝑎𝑛−2 | + ⋯ + |𝑎1 | + |𝑎0 | < 1. Then the polynomial equation has a root residing in 𝐷2 = {𝑧 ∈ ℂ ∣ |𝑧| ⩽ 1}. Step 4: General case. Let 𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0 be a polynomial equation with coefficients in ℂ. Then the polynomial equation has at least one complex root. We will need the following lemma to aid us with the second and third step in the proof of Theo- rem 1.2.

items.label: Nullhomotopy Criterion via the Induced Fundamental Group Homomorphism
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let ℎ ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑋 be a continuous map such that ℎ(1) = 𝑥0 . Then ℎ is nullhomotopic if and only if the induced homomorphism ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is the trivial homomorphism.

Proof. Assume that ℎ is nullhomotopic. Specifically, let ℎ ≃ 𝑐 where 𝑐 is the constant map 𝑐 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑋 given by 𝑐(𝑧) = 𝑥. Then by Lemma 7.37 there is a path 𝛼 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑋 from 𝑥0 to 𝑥 such that 𝑐∗ = 𝛼̂◦ℎ∗ . Since 𝛼 ̂ is an isomorphism (cf. the proof of Theorem 7.19), it follows that since 𝑐∗ is the trivial homomorphism, then so is ℎ∗ . Now assume that ℎ∗ is the trivial homomorphism. Let 𝛾 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 be the loop in 𝑆 1 based at 1 given by 𝛾(𝑠) = 𝑒2𝜋𝑖𝑠 . As the group of integers (with addition as its binary operation) is cyclic, i.e., ℤ = ⟨1⟩, cf. Example B.10, and 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) ≅ ℤ, it follows that [𝛾] is a generator for 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1). Since ℎ∗ is the trivial homomorphism, it follows that ℎ∗ ([𝛾]) = [ℎ◦𝛾] = 𝑒 ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) where 𝑒 is the identity element. Thus there is a path homotopy 𝐹 ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 from ℎ◦𝛾 to 𝑐𝑥0 where 𝑐𝑥0 is the constant path in 𝑋 at 𝑥0 . In particular, 𝐹 (0, 𝑡) = 𝐹 (1, 𝑡) = 𝑥0 for all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. We want to show that there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑆 1 × 𝐼 → 𝑋 from ℎ to the constant map 𝑐 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑋 given by 𝑐(𝑧) = 𝑥0 . Since 𝛾 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 is a surjective continuous map whose domain is compact and whose codomain is Hausdorff, it follows that it is a quotient map. Thus 𝛾 × id ∶ 𝐼 × 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 × 𝐼 is also a quotient map where id ∶ 𝐼 → 𝐼 is the identity map. Then 𝐹 induces a continuous map 𝐻 ∶ 𝑆 1 × 𝐼 → 𝑋, where 𝐹 = 𝐻◦(𝛾 × id), that is a homotopy from ℎ to 𝑐. Hence, ℎ is nullhomotopic.

Chapter 8. The fundamental group of the circle 91

We now have everything we need to prove Theorem 1.2.

Proof of Theorem 1.2. Step 1: Let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑆 1 be the map given by 𝑓 (𝑧) = 𝑧𝑛 , and let 𝛾 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 be the loop in 𝑆 1 based at 1 given by 𝛾(𝑠) = 𝑒2𝜋𝑖𝑠 . Then the loop 𝑓 ◦𝛾 ∶ 𝐼 → 𝑆 1 , given by ( )𝑛 (𝑓 ◦𝛾)(𝑠) = 𝑓 (𝛾(𝑠)) = 𝑒2𝜋𝑖𝑠 = 𝑒2𝜋𝑖𝑛𝑠 ,

lifts to the path 𝜃 ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ, given by 𝜃(𝑠) = 𝑛𝑠, as

(𝑝◦𝜃)(𝑠) = 𝑝(𝜃(𝑠)) = 𝑝(𝑛𝑠) = 𝑒2𝜋𝑖𝑛𝑠 = (𝑓 ◦𝛾)(𝑠)

where 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 is the map given by 𝑝(𝑡) = 𝑒2𝜋𝑖𝑡 which we know to be a covering map, cf. Theo- rem 8.6. Let Φ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → ℤ be the lifting correspondence derived from 𝑝, cf. Theorem 8.16. Then

Φ([𝑓 ◦𝛾]) = 𝜃(1) = 𝑛

and as 𝛾 lifts to the path 𝛾̃ ∶ 𝐼 → ℝ given by 𝛾̃(𝑠) = 𝑠, we have

Φ([𝛾]) = 𝛾̃(1) = 1.

By definition of the induced homomorphism 𝑓∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1), we have

𝑓∗ ([𝛾]) = [𝑓 ◦𝛾].

Since Φ([𝑓 ◦𝛾]) = 𝑛 this means that if [𝛼], [𝛽] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) where 𝑓∗ ([𝛼]) = [𝛽], we have Φ([𝛽]) = 𝑛Φ([𝛼]) (here we use the fact that 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) is cyclic and generated by [𝛾]), and so, Φ(𝑓∗ ([𝛼])) = 𝑛Φ([𝛼]). In this sense, we can speak of 𝑓∗ as “multiplication by 𝑛.” We want to show that 𝑓∗ is injective. Let [𝛼1 ], [𝛼2 ] ∈ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) such that [𝛼1 ] ≠ [𝛼2 ]. Since Φ is bijective, and hence, injective, it follows that Φ([𝛼1 ]) ≠ Φ([𝛼2 ]). Furthermore, since 𝑛 ∈ ℤ+ we have

Φ(𝑓∗ ([𝛼1 ])) = 𝑛Φ([𝛼1 ]) ≠ 𝑛Φ([𝛼2 ]) = Φ(𝑓∗ ([𝛼2 ])).

Thus [𝛼1 ] ≠ [𝛼2 ] implies that Φ(𝑓∗ ([𝛼1 ])) ≠ Φ(𝑓∗ ([𝛼2 ])). In other words, 𝑓∗ is injective. Step 2: Let 𝑔 ∶ 𝑆 1 → ℂ ⧵ {0} be the map given by 𝑔(𝑧) = 𝑧𝑛 . Then if 𝑗 ∶ 𝑆 1 → ℂ ⧵ {0} is the inclusion map, we have 𝑔 = 𝑗◦𝑓 . By Example 7.29 and Lemma 7.30, the induced homomorphism 𝑗∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → 𝜋1 (ℂ ⧵ {0}, 1) is a monomorphism, and by Theorem 7.24 so is 𝑔∗ as

𝑔∗ = (𝑗◦𝑓 )∗ = 𝑗∗ ◦𝑓∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → 𝜋1 (ℂ ⧵ {0}, 1)

i.e., 𝑔∗ is the composition of two monomorphisms. As 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) ≅ ℤ, the monomorphism 𝑔∗ cannot be the trivial homomorphism. Thus by Lemma 8.18 𝑔 is not nullhomotopic. Step 3: Consider the polynomial equation given by

𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0

where we assume that the coefficients 𝑎0 , 𝑎1 , … 𝑎𝑛−1 are complex numbers and that

|𝑎𝑛−1 | + |𝑎𝑛−2 | + ⋯ + |𝑎1 | + |𝑎0 | < 1.

92 8.3. The fundamental theorem of algebra

Assume that the polynomial equation has no root in 𝐷2 . We will show that this leads to a contradiction to the fact that the map 𝑔 defined in the previous step is not nullhomotopic. By assumption that the polynomial equation has no root in 𝐷2 , we may define a map 𝑘 ∶ 𝐷2 → ℂ ⧵ {0} given by 𝑘(𝑧) = 𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 . Then, clearly, 𝑘 is continuous. Let ℎ = 𝑘|𝑆 1 . Then ℎ = 𝑘◦𝑖 where 𝑖 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝐷2 is the inclusion map. Since ℎ∗ = (𝑘◦𝑖)∗ = 𝑘∗ ◦𝑖∗ must be the trivial homomorphism, it follows that ℎ is nullhomotopic by Lemma 8.18. We now show that there is a homotopy from 𝑔 to ℎ, and so, 𝑔 must be nullhomotopic as ℎ is null- homotopic. This contradicts our findings from the previous step, and hence, the polynomial equation

𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0

where we assume that the coefficients 𝑎0 , 𝑎1 , … , 𝑎𝑛−1 are complex numbers and that

|𝑎𝑛−1 | + |𝑎𝑛−2 | + ⋯ + |𝑎1 | + |𝑎0 | < 1

must have a root in 𝐷2 . Let 𝐹 ∶ 𝑆 1 × 𝐼 → ℂ ⧵ {0} be the map given by

𝐹 (𝑧, 𝑡) = 𝑧𝑛 + 𝑡(𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + 𝑎𝑛−2 𝑧𝑛−2 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 ).

Then 𝐹 is clearly continuous and 𝐹 (𝑧, 0) = 𝑧𝑛 = 𝑔(𝑧) and 𝐹 (𝑧, 1) = 𝑧𝑛 +𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 +⋯+𝑎1 𝑧+𝑎0 = ℎ(𝑧) for all 𝑧 ∈ 𝑆 1 . Note that since 𝑔(𝑧) ≠ 0 for all 𝑧 ∈ 𝑆 1 and ℎ(𝑧) ≠ 0 for all 𝑧 ∈ 𝑆 1 . Since

|𝐹 (𝑧, 𝑡)| = |𝑧𝑛 + 𝑡(𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + 𝑎𝑛−2 𝑧𝑛−2 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 )| ⩾ |𝑧𝑛 | − |𝑡(𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + 𝑎𝑛−2 𝑧𝑛−2 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 )| ⩾ |𝑧𝑛 | − 𝑡(|𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 | + |𝑎𝑛−2 𝑧𝑛−2 | + ⋯ + |𝑎1 𝑧| + |𝑎0 |) = 1 − 𝑡(|𝑎𝑛−1 | + |𝑎𝑛−2 | + ⋯ + |𝑎1 | + |𝑎0 |)

where the two inequalities follow from the triangle inequality and the following equality follows from the fact that |𝑧| = 1 as 𝑧 ∈ 𝑆 1 , it follows from the assumption that

|𝑎𝑛−1 | + |𝑎𝑛−2 | + ⋯ + |𝑎1 | + |𝑎0 | < 1

and that 0 < 𝑡 < 1 that |𝐹 (𝑧, 𝑡)| > 0 for all 𝑧 ∈ 𝑆 1 and all 0 < 𝑡 < 1. Hence, 𝐹 is a homotopy from 𝑔 to ℎ. Step 4: For the final step we need to show that we can extend from the special case in the previous step to the general case. Let 𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0 be a polynomial equation with complex coefficients. In order to use the previous step we need to “scale” the coefficients such that we have a similar assumption of the sum of the modulus of the coefficients as in the previous step. To do this, choose a (real) number 𝑠 > 0 large enough so that | 𝑎𝑛−1 | | 𝑎𝑛−2 | | 𝑎1 | | 𝑎0 𝑠 | + | 𝑠2 | + ⋯ + | 𝑠𝑛−1 | + | 𝑠𝑛 | < 1. Let 𝑧 = 𝑠𝑤 such that the polynomial equation

𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0

Chapter 8. The fundamental group of the circle 93

now becomes 𝑠𝑛 𝑤𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑠𝑛−1 𝑤𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑠𝑤 + 𝑎0 = 0. Dividing by 𝑠𝑛 we get the polynomial equation 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑛−1 𝑎1 𝑎 𝑤𝑛 + 𝑤 + ⋯ + 𝑛−1 𝑤 + 𝑛0 = 0 𝑠 𝑠 𝑠 which, by the previous step, must have a root, say, 𝑤0 in 𝐷2 . Hence, 𝑧0 = 𝑠𝑤0 is a root for the polynomial equation 𝑧𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛−1 𝑧𝑛−1 + ⋯ + 𝑎1 𝑧 + 𝑎0 = 0. This completes the proof.

The property that every complex non-constant polynomial has a complex root is often referred to as the (field of) complex numbers being algebraically closed. The field of real numbers is, however, not algebraically closed, as, e.g., the polynomial equation 𝑥2 + 1 = 0 has no root in ℝ.

8.4 Exercises

Exercise 8.1 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Show that the product space ℝ × 𝑆 1 is a covering space of the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 .

Exercise 8.2 Let 𝐵 be a simply connected space, and let 𝐸 be a path-connected space. Show that if 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map, then 𝑝 is a homeomorphism.

Exercise 8.3 Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑒0 ∈ 𝐸. Show that if 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map such that 𝑝(𝑒0 ) = 𝑏0 , then the induced homomorphism 𝑝∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝐸, 𝑒0 ) → 𝜋1 (𝐵, 𝑏0 ) is a monomorphism.

Exercise 8.4 Let 𝐵 be a Hausdorff space, and let 𝐸 be a topological space. Show that if 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map, then 𝐸 must be Hausdorff.

Exercise 8.5 Let 𝑆 1 be the unit circle considered as a subspace of the complex numbers ℂ where ℂ ≅ ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Show that for every integer 𝑛 greater than 1 the map 𝑝 ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑆 1 given by 𝑝(𝑧) = 𝑧𝑛 is a covering map.

Exercise 8.6 Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let ℤ denote the set of integers. Consider 𝐸 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∈ ℝ2 ∣ 𝑥 − 𝑦 ∈ ℤ} as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Show that the map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → ℝ given by 𝑝(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑥 is a covering map.

94 8.4. Exercises

Exercise 8.7 Let 𝑛 be a positive integer that is greater than or equal to 2. Show that for any 𝑥0 ∈ ℝP𝑛 𝜋1 (ℝP𝑛 , 𝑥0 ) ≅ ℤ∕2 where ℝP𝑛 is real projective 𝑛-space. (You may assume as a known fact that the 𝑛-sphere 𝑆 𝑛 (considered as a subspace of ℝ𝑛+1 where ℝ𝑛+1 is given the standard topology) is simply con- nected for 𝑛 ⩾ 2.)

Exercise 8.8 Let { √ } 2 | | 𝐴𝑎,𝑏 = (𝑥1 , 𝑥2 ) ∈ ℝ | 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑥21 + 𝑥22 ⩽ 𝑏 | and consider it as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology and 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ ℝ with 0 < 𝑎 < 1 < 𝑏. Compute 𝜋1 (𝐴𝑎,𝑏 , 𝑥0 ) for any 𝑥0 ∈ 𝐴𝑎,𝑏 .

A. Set theory

The purpose of this appendix is not to give a complete and precise introduction to set theory but rather to introduce some of the basic ideas, notation and terminologies from set theory that will be used sets and operations on sets using natural language. The reader who is interested in a more thorough introduction to set theory may wish to consult [4, Chapter 1] or [5, Appendix A]. For an even more extensive treatment of set theory, see, e.g., [6].

A.1 Sets In our naive approach, a set is any collection of mathematical objects, e.g., prime numbers. These mathematical objects will be referred to as the elements of the set. We will typically denote sets using uppercase letters, e.g., 𝑋, and elements of sets with lowercase letters, e.g., 𝑥. If 𝑋 is a set and 𝑥 an element of 𝑋, we write 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑥 is not an element of 𝑋, we write 𝑥 ∉ 𝑋. In this notation, the symbol “∈” denotes membership in a set. We will use the symbols ℕ, ℚ, ℝ and ℤ to denote the sets of natural numbers, rational numbers, real numbers and integers, respectively. We will use the convention that 0 is a natural number. We will use ℤ+ to denote the set of positive integers, i.e., integers greater than or equal to 1. Sets may be specified by listing its elements, e.g.,

𝑃 = {Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune}

for the set of planets in the Solar System. We may also specify sets by selecting the elements of some previously given set that satisfy some well-defined condition, e.g.,

𝑆 = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑥 ⩾ 10.9}

read as “the set of 𝑥 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑥 ⩾ 10.9.” In this example, 129.3 ∈ 𝑆 while −1.5 ∉ 𝑆. Note that some authors use colon instead of ∣ when describing sets in this way. A set consisting of exactly one element is referred to as a singleton. If {𝑥} is the singleton consisting of only the element 𝑥, then 𝑥 ∈ {𝑥} but 𝑥 is not equal to {𝑥} as the former is one object whereas the latter is a set (consisting of one element) and these are not the same mathematical objects. We will refer to the set of no elements as the empty set and denote it by ∅. If a set 𝑆 has one or more elements, we will say that 𝑆 is non-empty.
items.formal_statement: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let ℎ ∶ 𝑆 1 → 𝑋 be a continuous map such that ℎ(1) = 𝑥0 . Then ℎ is nullhomotopic if and only if the induced homomorphism ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (𝑆 1 , 1) → 𝜋1 (𝑋, 𝑥0 ) is the trivial homomorphism.
items.intuition: Records an auxiliary result about nullhomotopy criterion via the induced fundamental group homomorphism that supports later statements or proofs.
